issue_title: Encoder will emit invalid Nones
issue_body:
```
>>> toml.dumps({'title': [ None ]})
'title = [ None,]\n'
```
This is not valid TOML, since TOML does not allow barewords (and indeed doesn't have any concept of null/none anyway). Sure enough,
```
>>> toml.loads(toml.dumps({'title': [ None ]}))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/tom/dev/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 395, in loads
    multibackslash)
  File "/home/tom/dev/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 587, in load_line
    value, vtype = self.load_value(pair[1], strictly_valid)
  File "/home/tom/dev/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 661, in load_value
    return (self.load_array(v), "array")
  File "/home/tom/dev/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 767, in load_array
    nval, ntype = self.load_value(a[i])
  File "/home/tom/dev/toml/toml/decoder.py", line 686, in load_value
    raise ValueError("This float doesn't have a leading "
ValueError: This float doesn't have a leading digit
```
It's invalid markup, so it's correct to error out, though the error message is a bit obtuse. I'd guess the correct answer is just to explicitly fail on encoding array-of-None.

issue_title: Open Project menu and directory tree browsing
issue_body:
I have few comments below regarding the File menu and the directory tree browser - don't know if they are easy to fix or not, but would improve the usability of your great software! 

The File menu has New Project option, but there is no obvious way to open an existing Project... The "Open directory" offers to convert non-Project folders to Project, and opens the Project ones, however right click the folder  on the directory tree offers only "New Directory" and "Delete" - "Create/Open Project" would be very logical here!.. Also the "New Directory" should be changed to "New Folder"?..

I understand that once a Project is created, it can be reopened from the history on the bottom of File menu. However I have URH folder tree on my OneDrive, which I'm accessing from several different computers - the project created on one PC will not appear on the URH history list of another PC. An easy way to just Open Project, preferably directly from the directory tree, would help a lot. BTW, just double clicking the folder on the directory tree, even the Project folder, should not open the Project - I'm just browsing, and right-click "Open Project" when I need...

Also, once you select a folders using "Open directory" (should it be renamed to "Open Folder", for consistency with "Convert Folder to Project" below?..), there is no way to browse up the tree, even if you opt not to convert the folder to project...

Is it also possible to add a horizontal scroll to the directory tree? In many cases it's only necessary to see the right-most part of the folder/file name, and expanding the directory tree is stealing the limited screen estate.

Thank you.

issue_title: missing response header raises error rather than failure
issue_body:
The following reports an error when it should report a failure instead:

```yaml
tests:

  - name: failure
    url: http://google.com

    status: 302
    response_headers:
        x-foo: bar
```

AFAICT that's because internally a `KeyError` is raised ("'x-foo' header not available in response keys: dict_keys(...)"): `testtools.TestCase`'s default `exception_handlers` doesn't have a mapping for that particular exception, so it defaults to `_report_error` rather than using `_report_failure`.

issue_title: Inferior fails to launch on openSUSE Leap 42.2 due to libtinfo not providing _nc_outch_sp
issue_body:
##### Describe the issue

On openSUSE Leap 42.2, the inferior debugger fails to launch. The symptom is the following (in the console pane of the gdbgui GUI):

    gdbgui spawned subprocess with pid 21941 from executable gdb.
    gdb process 21941 is running for this tab
    The underlying gdb process has been killed. This tab will no longer function as expected.

This happens every time gdbgui is launched, regardless of arguments.

##### Steps to replicate

* Launch gdbgui on the command line.
* Navigate to 127.0.0.1:5000
* The message(s) appear(s) immediately in the console pane. No debugging is possible.

##### Describe your environment
* Distribution: openSUSE Leap 42.2, 
* `uname -a`: `Linux <hostname> 4.4.104-18.44-default #1 SMP Thu Jan 4 08:07:55 UTC 2018 (05a9de6) x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux`
* gdbgui version (`gdbgui -v`): 0.11.1.2
* gdb version (`gdb -v`): 8.0.1
* browser: tested in Firefox 52.6.0 and Chrome Version 65.0.3325.146 (Official Build). The problem is likely not related to the browser.
* python packages (`pip freeze`): N/A as I'm using the standalone binary.

##### Investigation

`strace`ing the binary reveals the following information. These are the relevant excerpts from the forked process that `exec`s the debugger:

    execve("/usr/bin/gdb", ["/usr/bin/gdb", "--interpreter=mi2"], [<environment info stripped .......>]
    open("/tmp/_MEImWZSco/libncursesw.so.5", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
    open("/lib64/libncursesw.so.5", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
    open("/tmp/_MEImWZSco/libtinfo.so.5", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
    writev(2, [{"/usr/bin/gdb", 12}, {": ", 2}, {"symbol lookup error", 19}, {": ", 2}, {"/lib64/libncursesw.so.5", 23}, {": ", 2}, {"undefined symbol: _nc_outch_sp", 30}, {"", 0}, {"", 0}, {"\n", 1}], 10) = 91
    exit_group(127)                         = ?

(I have stripped much for legibility, but I have the full logs available.) Note how it uses the `libncursesw` library from my system, but the `libtinfo` shipped with gdbgui. However, my system `libncursesw` requires the following symbol:

    $ eu-readelf -s /lib64/libncursesw.so.5
    Symbol table [ 4] '.dynsym' contains 661 entries:
     1 local symbol  String table: [ 5] '.dynstr'
      Num:            Value   Size Type    Bind   Vis          Ndx Name
       37: 0000000000000000      0 FUNC    GLOBAL DEFAULT    UNDEF _nc_outch_sp

It is provided by my system's `libtinfo` library:

    $ eu-readelf -s /lib64/libtinfo.so.5
    Symbol table [ 4] '.dynsym' contains 315 entries:
     1 local symbol  String table: [ 5] '.dynstr'
      Num:            Value   Size Type    Bind   Vis          Ndx Name
      206: 000000000001aaf0    187 FUNC    GLOBAL DEFAULT       13 _nc_outch_sp

but not by the one shipped with `gdbgui`, i.e.,

    $ eu-readelf -s /tmp/_MEIQ2ccQj/libtinfo.so.5  | grep _nc_outch_sp

returns nothing.

    $ rpm -qf /lib64/libncursesw.so.5 /lib64/libtinfo.so.5
    libncurses5-5.9-55.9.1.x86_64
    libncurses5-5.9-55.9.1.x86_64

I wasn't able to figure out the version of ncurses that was shipped with gdbgui.

issue_title: 标准行情api.get_security_quotes不能同时混合获取股票与其他品种
issue_body:
标准行情api.get_security_quotes除了股票，也可以获取交易的可转债等品种实时数据，但发现有以下限制：
1，单独一个代码的列表，可以获取到数据。比如’502027‘。
2，如果这个代码是混合在一个股票列表里面，就可能出错。
3，这个代码如果是在列表的第一个或者最后一个，有时不会报错。
这么古怪的问题，不清楚是我本地问题/不同券商服务器策略问题还是TDX天生的限制

注意点：非股票品种的债券等获取的价格为实际价格*10。这是TDX为了防止浮点数错误，报价在传输和存储时实际都保存为整数，然后根据品种进行处理的结果。

issue_title: Feature Req: 'Pythonicifiy' dbus integer attributes that are bitfields or enums
issue_body:
There is a long standing custom in C programming, that has bled into dbus, that uses integer return values to not represent integers but to mean what python does with (possibly read-only) sets.  In C and hence dbus, we often see a collection of single bits raised to published fixed powers of 2 'ored' together then returned as an integer.  And the inverse when accepting parameter arguments.

Likewise a search of nearly any C interface, and hence dbus, will define an integer to not mean an integer, but either an offset in to a C 'enumerated type', or, worse, one of a collection of '#define X_Y Z' statements where X is what python would understand as the name of a variable restricted to a member of a tuple,  Y the state of X should the integer == Z.  In python just a member of the tuple, or possibly set.

To avoid many instances of 'cruft' in the form of decorators and subclassing for that purpose,  perhaps pydbus would add a method that would accept a bunch of tuples that contain a regular expression to be matched against _path and a collection of attributes associated with translation instructions, and a method such as _translated(attribute) to and _translate(attribute,value) to set them then call the underlying integer based method.

Or, well, something pythonic.  As it is, there will be a great deal of repetitive code being generated by pydbus users to do one-of versions of the foregoing.

I've added a couple entries to the wiki to allow users of 'long term support' distros access to pydbus, as well as a roadmap to get the latest python, and pydbus without needed elevated privileges on any distro with an example for centos 7.

issue_title: Question: to_compact for Measurements
issue_body:
I guess this is somewhat related to #350, #566.

A `Quantity` object has the method `to_compact`, however if using a `Measurement` instance, the inherited `to_compact` method returns without any formatting because the magnitude is a ` uncertainties.ufloat` which is not a `numbers.Number` type object. This is caused by the [following lines](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/quantity.py#L432):

```python
if not isinstance(self.magnitude, numbers.Number):
    msg = ("to_compact applied to non numerical types "
           "has an undefined behavior.")
    w = RuntimeWarning(msg)
    warnings.warn(w, stacklevel=2)
    return self
```

demo of behavior (`Python 3.6.0 (v3.6.0:41df79263a11, Dec 23 2016, 08:06:12) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)] on win32` running on `win7x64 pro`): 
```python
# current behavior
>>> import pint
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> M_ = ureg.Measurement
>>> m1 = M_(10, 0.5, ureg.ohm)*1e3
>>> m1.to_compact()
__main__:1: RuntimeWarning: to_compact applied to non numerical types has an undefined behavior.
<Measurement(10000.00, 500.00, ohm)>
```
Unfortunately I found the [docs](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/docs/measurement.rst) less than helpful in this case. Am I missing something on how to get the following output?:
```python
# desirable behavior
>>> import pint
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> M_ = ureg.Measurement
>>> m1 = M_(10, 0.5, ureg.ohm)*1e3
>>> m1.to_compact()  #  what method to call?
<Measurement(10.00, 0.50, kiloohm)>
```

If there is no built in method/support for this I think it shouldn't be too hard to implement as a new/overridden method in the `Measurement` class.
The major issue with this approach that comes to mind is:
+ as in #566, if the `Quantity.to_compact` method changes behavior, then this new `Measurement.to_compact` method will have to be modified as well to mirror that behavior in order to provide a uniform experience.

 I wouldn't mind sitting down and taking a crack at it. But before I go poking too far into things I haven't fully read through, I figured I would ask if I was just missing the correct way of presenting Measurements in a more convenient to understand form, as just display formatting will not perform the unit conversion that is desired here. 

It looks like the `Measurement` class will be getting an overhaul coming soon so if this is something that is already planned then great. I've recently taken in interest in including tolerances/variances in demo examples to illustrate more realistic system models and pint/uncertainties has been an intuitive and flexible answer to the goal thus far.

issue_title: Recursion error when creating a string from an ICMP packet.
issue_body:
I'm using the following code to build an ICMP packet.
```
def build_icmp_echo(id=0, seq=0, data=''):
    echo = dpkt.icmp.ICMP.Echo()
    echo.id = id
    echo.seq = seq
    echo.data = data

    icmp = dpkt.icmp.ICMP()
    icmp.type = dpkt.icmp.ICMP_ECHO
    icmp.data = str(data)

    return icmp
```
When I call str() on the ICMP object I get the following stack trace:
```
 File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dpkt/dpkt.py", line 141, in __str__
    return str(self.__bytes__())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dpkt/icmp.py", line 130, in __bytes__

The stack trace continues like this until a max recursion depth error is reached. The `ICMP.__bytes__()` method is calling the `dpkt.Packet.__str__()` method, which calls the `ICMP.__bytes__()` method. issue_title: showing a AtomGroup loses local changes
issue_body:
When I show a AtomGroup the coordinates are reloaded from the trajectory. If I made any local changes for modeling or analysis they are lost and I can't check if any of my transformation had the desired effect. To reproduce this see code below.

```python
import MDAnalysis as mda
from MDAnalysisTests.datafiles import PSF, DCD
import nglview as ng

u = mda.Universe(PSF, DCD)
p = u.select_atoms('all')

# change into principle axes frame
pa = p.principle_axes()
p.translate(-p.center_of_geometry())
p.rotate(pa)

p.write('psf_in_pa.pdb')

# this will show the protein with the original loaded coordinates
view = ng.show_mdanalysis(p)
view

# now the protein is shown in the principal axes coordinates system
u2 = mda.Universe('psf_in_pa.pdb')
view2 = ng.show_mdanalysis(u2)
view2
```

I'm aware this is mostly a problem of MDAnalysis and the  current behavior is good to look at a trajectory. To look at a single AtomGroup this isn't ideal though. Adding a new keyword to `show_mdanalysis` to not reload the coordinates would be good solution.

issue_title: Add DateTime.get_days(), DateTime.get_weeks(), DateTime.get_weekday()
issue_body:
`DateTime.get_days()` --> Should return the total number of days between the given dates. For example, `DateTime.get_days('05/23/2016', '06/23/2016')` should return you the total number of days between those two dates.
`DateTime.get_weeks()` --> Same as `get_days()` but, this returns total number of weeks between the given dates.
`DateTime.get_weekday()` --> Should return a tuple something like `(3, 'Wednesday')` when a date is given. For example, `DateTime.get_weekday('8/3/2016')` should return `(3, 'Wednesday')` where 3 represents `third day in the week`.

issue_title: [Feature request] Connection pool
issue_body:
Excerpt from the [official docs](https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/NetworkingInternet/Conceptual/RemoteNotificationsPG/CommunicatingwithAPNs.html) on _Best Practices for Managing Connections_,

> **Keep your connections with APNs open across multiple notifications; do not repeatedly open and close connections. APNs treats rapid connection and disconnection as a denial-of-service attack**. You should leave a connection open unless you know it will be idle for an extended period of time—for example, if you only send notifications to your users once a day, it is acceptable practice to use a new connection each day.

I think it's the best for _apns2-client_ library to conform to this best practice, in order to avoid the possibility of having the services built with the library being seeing as a denial-of-service attack and thus interruption to those services. Therefore, I think we should add a connection pool for managing the reuse of open connections to this library.

Thanks for developing this library. It has been working great for me and its code is easy to read and follow.

issue_title: Automatic versioning breaks if mercurial repository is synced with a GIT upstream through hg-git extension
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Armin Gruner (BitBucket: [ArminGruner](http://bitbucket.org/ArminGruner), GitHub: [ArminGruner](http://github.com/ArminGruner))**

----------------------------------------

Hi,
if a local hg repo is pushed/pulled towards a GIT repository through the hg-git extension, bookmarks are used to synchronize which branches are pushed upstream:

```
#!shell
» hg tags
tip                               99:989ec00b2a11
default/master                    99:989ec00b2a11
```

Unfortunately, this breaks the check in hgtools/versioning.py:

```
#!python

if tags == ['tip'] and not self.is_modified():
        tags = self.get_parent_tags('tip')
```

hgtools always assumes the local sandbox has been modified. I've changed the code to just check whether the sandbox version is the tip-most version if the reported tags contain the "tip":


```
#!python

if 'tip' in tags and not self.is_modified():
        tags = self.get_parent_tags('tip')
```

Not sure whether this does not break any corner case, though..

Regards
Armin

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/hgtools/issue/31

issue_title: Encoding with RSA public key and decoding with private key
issue_body:
In the documentation it says:
> When using the RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5 algorithms, the key argument in both jwt.encode() and jwt.decode() ("secret" in the examples) is expected to be either an RSA public or private key in PEM format.

When i try to encode the payload with the public key i get an error:

    >>> jwt_message = jwt.encode(payload, pub_rsakey, 'RS384')
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<input>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/Users/sven/code/sso/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/__init__.py",
     line 295, in encode
        signature = signing_methods[algorithm](signing_input, key)
      File "/Users/sven/code/sso/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/__init__.py",
     line 131, in <lambda>
        'RS384': lambda msg, key: sign_rsa(msg, key, hashes.SHA384()),
      File "/Users/sven/code/sso/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/__init__.py",
     line 106, in sign_rsa
        signer = key.signer(
    AttributeError: '_RSAPublicKey' object has no attribute 'signer'

What is happening is that the implementation in [algorithms.py, line 137](https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/master/jwt/algorithms.py#L137) tries to sign with the public key, but the `cryptography.hazmat.backends.openssl.rsa._RSAPublicKey` has no `signer` method: https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/primitives/interfaces/#cryptography.hazmat.primitives.interfaces.RSAPublicKey

issue_title: Texture indices not aligned to face indices
issue_body:
While parsing an OBJ file, the texture coordinates are indexed immediately: https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh/blob/952d3807849646595fb892d39d75ec8010c3b157/trimesh/io/wavefront.py#L87-L89
and then put in the metadata:
https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh/blob/952d3807849646595fb892d39d75ec8010c3b157/trimesh/io/wavefront.py#L44-L46
The vertex coordinates and the faces are passed to Trimesh object. Later _validate_face_normals and update_faces are called that mixes up the face indices. Due to this the face indices not match the texture indices (that has been already indexed), obviously it renders a bad texture:
![bug](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15861028/36079625-80949478-0f85-11e8-853b-1aca666aae6b.png)

Not sure what would be an easy way to keep the changes of the face indices the same with the texture coordinate indices. It could start by storing the texture coordinates and texture coordinate indices separately in metadata.

issue_title: Allow modifying the socket chunk-size
issue_labels: [{'id': 443713392, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0NDM3MTMzOTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/exhuma/puresnmp/labels/Difficulty:%20Trivial', 'name': 'Difficulty: Trivial', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 440137563, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0NDAxMzc1NjM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/exhuma/puresnmp/labels/Type:%20Enhancement', 'name': 'Type: Enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}, {'id': 957302478, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NTczMDI0Nzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/exhuma/puresnmp/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c0ef99', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
Currently the chunk-size for socket reading is hardcoded to `4096` bytes. This value could be made configurable for the end-user. This is *kind* of related to #45 as it may reduce the chance of running into #45. However, fixing #45 has priority!

This is also related to #1 as it is another configuration value for the network behaviour.

Currently the `timeout` value is exposed by most top-level functions. To guarantee backwards-compatibility a new argument `chunk_size` should be introduced.

For the next major release, this would make more sense in the creation of an SNMP-Client instance (see also #3)

issue_title: [bug] --ignore E129 not working
issue_body:
# System Versions

```
$ autopep --version
autopep8 1.2.2
$ pep8 --version
1.7.0
$ python --version
Python 2.7.10
$ uname -a
Linux <hidden> 4.2.0-30-generic #36-Ubuntu SMP Fri Feb 26 00:58:07 UTC 2016 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

# Example input that caused the 'bug'

## `ex.py`:

```python
if (A and
    (B or C)):
    pass
```

## Console

```
$ pep8 ex.py
test.py:2:5: E129 visually indented line with same indent as next logical line
$ autopep8 ex.py
if (A and
        (B or C)):
    pass
$ autopep8 ex.py --ignore E129
if (A and
        (B or C)):
    pass
```

## Expected output

Given the `--ignore E129` positional argument, there shouldn't be any changes - meaning it should just be the initial `ex.py` with no fix in indentations

As one can see, regardless of `--ignore E129` or not, the `autopep8` just fixes the bad indentation.

The whole point of the E129 error was that the `pep8` team branched it off from E125, so we can disable this one specific pet peeve. Now I'm slightly annoyed by how the `--ignore E129` doesn't work.

I upgraded to the latest version, which is `1.2.2`, so can anyone confirm this bug?

issue_title: Winston Lutz Accuracy
issue_body:
Hi All, 

I've encountered a situation where the Winston-Lutz optimization didn't act as expected, and on further investigation, I wanted to discuss with the wider community. 

I gave the algorithm four images at cardinal gantry angles (0, 90, 180, 270) from an old linac with a radiation isocentre diameter in the 1-2mm range. The G0 and G180 images gave CAX positions which differed relative to the BB by ~1.2mm, and the lateral images differed by ~0.5mm. 

The algorithm works by minimizing the maximum distance from the radiation isocentre point to any line it finds. Given four images like that, once the radiation isocentre point is located between the two projections with the larger distance between them, there is nothing to push the optimization to minimize the distance to the projections with smaller distances between them - in my case the algorithm essentially ignored these projections. See the image below - the red dot is the calculated radiation isocentre, the blue cross is the BB, the red and orange lines are the AP projections and the green and blue lines are the lateral projections. 

![wl](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/943852/29117812-91bd7916-7cf7-11e7-8a5e-66e266194390.png)

Would it be more appropriate if the minimization was looking to reduce the average distance?

issue_title: fails to get local interfaces if cannot resolve local hostname
issue_body:
Hey,
I noticed that for some reason, SSHTunnelForwarder would always throw an exception regardless of the parameters, 

  socket.gaierror: [Errno 8] nodename nor servname provided, or not known

it was caused by _get_local_interfaces trying to resolve the local hostname (in my case, Ich) and failing (probably just a bad /etc/hosts file or something, not really sure)

anyway, this caused me a couple of hours of frustration but i eventually figured out that if i replace 

          local_if = socket.gethostbyname_ex(socket.gethostname())[-1]

with 

          try:
            local_if = socket.gethostbyname_ex(socket.gethostname())[-1]
        except:
            local_if = []

it will handle this exception.

I can make a pull request if you like.

issue_title: Implementation of the SELECT parameter
issue_body:
I've found that when I request results an alternative format (ris, bibtex) the date returned tends to be the published-online date (perhaps the earliest of the published-online & published-print). The authors I'm working with tend to cite the published-print date, so I'm looking for strategies to provide the print date instead. 

Examples:
http://api.crossref.org/works/10.1007/s10980-007-9188-1
ris date = published-online = 2007/12/22 (published-print = 2008/2)

http://api.crossref.org/works/ 10.1139/cjfr-2014-0148
ris date = published-print = 2015/2 (no published-online data)

One option would be to make an additional request to the API specifically for the published-print date using the SELECT parameter, and use it to replace the date in the RIS-formatted record. I don't think Habanero supports the SELECT parameter? I could perhaps modify the counts.py module to pick out the published-print info, but I see it uses a different url requiring a login, so I'm wondering if it might be better to try to implement the SELECT parameter in the cn.py code instead?

Another option would be to modify the crossref API's content negotiation / parsing code, but I don't see this available anywhere (I've only found this general reference: https://citation.crosscite.org/docs.html) (I realize this is more an issue for the crossref API forum).

issue_title: virtual_memory() on NetBSD
issue_body: virtual_memory() opened the /proc/meminfo file as a binary. As such, each line was a binary, but lines 135 and 137 were checking against a string type. This caused str.startswith() to raise a TypeError and fail.

issue_title: osx how to run from command line
issue_body:
Using the documentation as reference:
`# Bind to TCP host/port pair:
bjoern.run(wsgi_application, host, port)

# TCP host/port pair, enabling SO_REUSEPORT if available.
bjoern.run(wsgi_application, host, port, reuse_port=True)

# Bind to Unix socket:
bjoern.run(wsgi_application, 'unix:/path/to/socket')

# Bind to abstract Unix socket: (Linux only)
bjoern.run(wsgi_application, 'unix:@socket_name')
`
is assuming that you are in a python cmd line session and have imported bjoern.  This seems cumbersome.
I believe there is also an assumption that the wsgi_application has also been imported.  Is this true?  Also, how would you actually run this in a production, non-terminal, non-python session environment?

I mentioned this [in this reddit comment](https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/6z1jt3/slack_cli_for_productive_developers/dn2h3cw/) which I'm essentially copying here:

In `names.cli:25`, if a person's account gets deleted then the lookup of the name by id fails and raises a `slacker.Error: user_not_found`. A possible way to deal with this would be something like
```
    try:
        self.user_index[user_id] = slack.client().users.info(user_id).body['user']['name']

```

Dear K.Rakpong krub,

May I have a suggestion about using deepcut with user dictionary support ? 

According to your sample "โรงเรียน", there are many words which shouldn't be cut such as "ขี้เกียจ"

![d1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15793866/31120823-11ef4c7a-a860-11e7-973c-b50973edd307.png)

issue_title: Python 3.4 and 3.6 are not listed in setup.py as supported version
Escape sequences are unprocessed on Windows with TERM set
issue_body:
On Windows, when the environment variable `TERM` is set to any value, escape sequences will be printed unprocessed (no colored output). Concerning the values of `TERM`/the terminals where this happens, I'm still waiting for user feedback (see msiemens/PyGitUp#24).

According to `git bisect` this behaviour has been introduced in 961428e6c1c3a6855da5dedccfc418f295b54c25.

issue_title: KeyAlgorithmIdentifierId in asn1crypto.CMS doesn't include encryption mode OIDs
issue_body: When the payload being encrypted is not another cryptographic key, regular encryption is done instead of key wrapping (at least that is what openssl command line does). If you agree, please include the 'encryption mode' OIDs in the KeyEncryptionAlgorithmIdentifierId map e.g. `2.16.840.1.101.3.4.1.2 - id-aes128-CBC `

issue_title: Traceback not sent to logger, but directly to stderr
issue_body:
https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/b1ae45c10d46e881492b0fd37f0919cac6d79224/kafka/conn.py#L618

At the line above, BrokerConnection prints a traceback directly to stderr, ignoring the logger. This makes it hard to filter out the traceback. Right now, for me the traceback occasionally starts generating GBs of output while at the same time a Producer stops sending information to Kafka, although why this happens I'm not sure yet, and I don't yet have a minimal example (I think it has something to do with losing a network connection though).

I think a better implementation would probably be to remove the traceback, and add stack_info=True to the previous logging call, as I think it does roughly the same thing. Or just get rid of the traceback, if it's not providing value to the project.

I should be able to provide a pull request, but I don't know if it would be preferable to get rid of the traceback entirely or not.

issue_title: CompileUnit._parse_DIEs() misreads DIE size in rare instances
issue_body:
It appears that pyelftools is having trouble correctly parsing the DIEs of all of the Go executables that I've passed it so far.

For reproduction purposes, I've uploaded the object file I was using as well as a python script and shell script that will replicate the issue. Additionally, I've uploaded both the full output from both executions, as well as a trimmed version of each that begin where the issues first appears.

https://drive.google.com/file/d/0B393V_QOoZPjUU5rbkNpV19zREU/view?usp=sharing

One of the first things you'll notice is that the pyelftools output completes long before dwarfdump does. You may also notice that the DIE at offset `0x00016ce8`, according to dwarfdump, has a size of 17 bytes, while that same DIE according to pyelftools is 23 bytes. Because pyelftools is reading the wrong number of bytes, it interprets the following DIE incorrectly, which causes the next one to be misinterpreted, and so on. This chain of events continues until it reads the "DIE" at offset `0x16d02` after which it stops. So not only is pyelftools misreading these DIE, but it's also not fully parsing the list. 

I'm really not sure what could be causing this. Maybe someone else has some idea?

issue_title: Feature request:   `include:markdown-it`
issue_body:
* pypugjs version: Github version
* Python version: 3.5
* Operating System: Ubuntu

Hi there and thanks for the effort in maintaining a very useful library.

There is a feature in Pug allowing to ``include`` a file while filtering its content.
It allows to import files in formats other than Pug, for instance markdown.
See [this page](https://pugjs.org/language/includes.html#including-filtered-text) for this example:

```
doctype html
html
  head
    title An Article
  body
    include:markdown-it article.md
```
Right now, this code in pypugjs gives a body with just a text ``article.md``. That's because pypugjs believes that ``include:markdown-it`` is a tag and ``article.md`` is a content.

Of course in the example above 'markdown-it' is not registered with pypugjs, but I also tried with a simple pre-registered filter ``capitalize`` and the result is the same

How difficult would it be to make this Pug feature work with pypugjs ? I am happy to help if you point me in the direction.

issue_title: Publishing while consuming
issue_body:
The worker should get new task from the _task_queue_, publish new message into the _task_queue2_ and acknowledge the received task from the _task_queue_.

Let's assume that the worker is not running. Now run _new_task.py_: 3 tasks were sent into the _task_queue_. Now run _worker.py_: 3 tasks were received from the _task_queue_, 3 tasks were sent into the _task_queue2_, BUT 1 task left **unacknowledged** in the _task_queue_. Can you help me to get to the reason, why? Maybe I am doing something wrong?

P.S.: Number of tasks in the new_task.py (default is 3) can be changed - **the higher this number is, the more unacknowledged tasks are left in the _task_queue_ after running the worker**.

**new_task.py**
```python
import asyncio
from aio_pika import connect, Message, DeliveryMode

async def main():
    connection = await connect("amqp://guest:guest@rabbit/")
    channel = await connection.channel()
    message_body = b"Hello World!"
    message = Message(message_body, delivery_mode=DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT)
    for _ in range(3):
        await channel.default_exchange.publish(message, routing_key='task_queue')
        print("Sent %r" % message)
    await connection.close()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

**worker.py**
```python
import asyncio
from aio_pika import connect, IncomingMessage, Message, DeliveryMode

channel = None

async def on_message(message):
    with message.process():
        print("Received message %r" % message.body)
        new_message = Message(b'New message', delivery_mode=DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT)
        await channel.default_exchange.publish(new_message, routing_key='task_queue2')

async def main():
    global channel
    connection = await connect("amqp://guest:guest@rabbit/")
    channel = await connection.channel()
    queue = await channel.declare_queue('task_queue', durable=True)
    await channel.declare_queue('task_queue2', durable=True)
    queue.consume(on_message)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.create_task(main())
loop.run_forever()
```

issue_title: feature request: halo ellipticity estimators
issue_body:
Similar to running an NG (tangential shear) correlation, but the user passes in an extra parameter, call it phi, for each object in the lens catalog. Treecorr returns the result of two optimally weighted estimators of the elliptical lensing shear, and two corresponding cross-components. All four results are arrays of length N, where N is the number of bins in angle (or perhaps projected scale in Mpc/h).

This extra input parameter phi is used to rotate the shear field for each lens into a common Cartesian coordinate system (gamma_1 and gamma_2 instead of gamma_+ and gamma_x). Having performed this rotation, the desired estimators to calculate are
gamma_4theta = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i * cos(4theta) + w_i * gamma_2i * sin(4theta)) / Sum_i (w_i)
gamma_const = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i) / Sum_i (w_i)

gamma_4theta_x = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_1i * cos(4theta) - w_i * gamma_2i * sin(4theta)) / Sum_i (w_i)
gamma_const_x = Sum_i (w_i * gamma_2i) / Sum_i (w_i)

where the index i runs over all lens-source pairs, and w_i is the usual weight for gamma (for example, sum of squares of measurement error and shape noise).

def _clean_data(data):
    """
    Clean a dictionary of data of potentially sensitive info before
    sending to the database.
    Function based on the "_clean_credentials" function of django
    (django/django/contrib/auth/__init__.py)
    """
    SENSITIVE_DATA = re.compile('api|token|key|secret|password|signature', re.I)

issue_title: Allow running all tests out of TravisPy account
Access ODBC queries with [ODBC;...].table references fail under pyodbc but work elsewhere (VBScript, .NET)
issue_body:
The Microsoft Access Database Engine allows us to include tables from an external database by prefixing the table name with the database location. If the database location is an ODBC data source the prefix is of the form

```sql
SELECT ... FROM [ODBC;DSN=mydsn].tablename
```

or

```sql
SELECT ... FROM [ODBC;DRIVER=...;SERVER=...;...].tablename
```

Testing shows that such queries work when called via Access ODBC from VBScript and .NET, but fail when called via Access ODBC using pyodbc.

Working VBScript code:

```vbs
Option Explicit
Dim conn_str_sql
conn_str_sql = "DRIVER=ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server;SERVER=127.0.0.1,52865;DATABASE=master;Trusted_Connection=yes;"
Dim conn_str_accdb
conn_str_accdb = "DRIVER={Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)};DBQ=C:\Users\Public\empty.accdb;"
Dim con
Set con = CreateObject("ADODB.Connection")
con.Open conn_str_accdb
Dim rst
Set rst = CreateObject("ADODB.Recordset")
Dim sql
sql = "SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM [ODBC;" + conn_str_sql + "].INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES"
WScript.Echo sql
rst.Open sql, con
WScript.Echo rst(0).Value
rst.Close
con.Close
```

Failing pyodbc code:

```python
import pyodbc
conn_str_sql = r"DRIVER=ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server;SERVER=127.0.0.1,52865;DATABASE=master;Trusted_Connection=yes;"
conn_str_accdb = r"DRIVER={Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)};DBQ=C:\Users\Public\empty.accdb;"
conn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str_accdb, autocommit=True)
crsr = conn.cursor()
sql = "SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM [ODBC;" + conn_str_sql + "].INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES"
print(sql)
crsr.execute(sql)
print(crsr.fetchval())
crsr.close()
conn.close()
```

The complete ODBC traces are attached. The difference in behaviour appears to be that shortly after SQLExecDirectW is called (line 640 in the vbs trace, line 192 in the Python trace) a call is made to SQLDriverConnectW which is failing under pyodbc.

That call returns SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO for vbs, as shown by the following excerpt starting at line 681 of the vbs trace ...

```none
cscript  odbc_d 15f8-9a8    ENTER SQLDriverConnectW 
        HDBC                0035C6C0
        HWND                00000000
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 [      -3] <Invalid string length!>
        SWORD                       -3 
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 
        SWORD                       -3 
        SWORD *             0x00000000
        UWORD                        1 <SQL_DRIVER_COMPLETE>

cscript  odbc_d 15f8-9a8    EXIT  SQLDriverConnectW  with return code 1 (SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO)
        HDBC                0035C6C0
        HWND                00000000
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 [      -3] <Invalid string length!>
        SWORD                       -3 
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 
        SWORD                       -3 
        SWORD *             0x00000000
        UWORD                        1 <SQL_DRIVER_COMPLETE>

        DIAG [IM006] [Microsoft][ODBC Driver Manager] Driver's SQLSetConnectAttr failed (0) 
```

... while the corresponding call from Python returns SQL_ERROR, as we see starting at line 233 of the Python trace:

```none
pythonw.exe -c  1318-1600   ENTER SQLDriverConnectW 
        HDBC                00149388
        HWND                00000000
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 [      -3] <Invalid string length!>
        SWORD                       -3 
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 
        SWORD                       -3 
        SWORD *             0x00000000
        UWORD                        1 <SQL_DRIVER_COMPLETE>

pythonw.exe -c  1318-1600   EXIT  SQLDriverConnectW  with return code -1 (SQL_ERROR)
        HDBC                00149388
        HWND                00000000
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 [      -3] <Invalid string length!>
        SWORD                       -3 
        WCHAR *             0x6E23B274 
        SWORD                       -3 
        SWORD *             0x00000000
        UWORD                        1 <SQL_DRIVER_COMPLETE>

        DIAG [S1110] [Microsoft][ODBC Driver Manager] Only SQL_DRIVER_NOPROMPT is allowed when connection pooling is enabled (0) 
```
[odbc_trace_vbs_success.txt](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/771163/odbc_trace_vbs_success.txt)
[odbc_trace_py_error.txt](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/771211/odbc_trace_py_error.txt)

issue_title: Invalid Cursor State when using executemany
issue_body:
When I try to insert loads of data with some columns (10-15) using executemany (and fast_executemany = True) I receive Invalid Cursor State (0) (SQLPrepare) error.
I suspect the params array being too big either for the driver, or for SQL Server or for pyodbc.
Is there any other way to "emulate" bcp?
Or is there any workaround/setting?

Environment:
- Python: 3.6.3 (64 bit)
- pyodbc: 4.0.21
- OS: Win7 (64 bit)
- DB: MSSQL 2014
- driver: ODBC 13

Edit:
After googling it, it seams, there is a limit of 1000 of the "values" clause.
is pyodbc using for executemany this statement: instert into mytable (c1, c2, ...) values (v1, v2, ...), (v1, v2, ...) ? even if fast_executemany is set to True?
How to overcome of this issue?
  
  Edit2:
It turned out, that the problem doesn't come from a limit issue.
After testing several use cases I've realized that if there is any cr.execute BEFORE cr.executemany than the cursor gets invalidated. 
One workaround is to use a different cursor for executemany or (re)declare the cursor before each usage (which is not too elegant)

issue_title: Calculating average quality score
issue_body:
Hi Wouter,

Thank you for your blog posts on how the average qscores are calculated for nanopore data, I have found them very useful.

When I tried out your aveQual function, I realised that I wasn't getting the correct outcome until I converted q to float, that is : 
-10*math.log(sum([10**(**float**(q)/-10) for q in quals]) / len(quals), 10)
It seems that if q is integer, then the exponent will be rounded up to an integer value.

Best regards,
Laura

issue_title: is it safe to use pyperclip?
issue_body:
Hello!

I have a question about this module, let me describe it:
it has been a while that I'm writing a password manager program. One of the problems I have faced, is how to send the password to user. An **unsafe** way is to print or show the password(why? visit [over the shoulder attack page in wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shoulder_surfing_(computer_security))) so, what to do? why not copying that password to clipboard, like other password managers? first, I wanted to use pyperclip, but I have also being interested in how does it work, so I read the source code in "pyperclip/\_\_init\_\_.py" . In it's header comment, at the end is written that:
> Security Note: This module runs programs with these names:
>    - which
>    - where
>    - pbcopy
>    - pbpaste
>    - xclip
>    - xsel
>    - klipper
>    - qdbus
> A malicious user could rename or add programs with these names, tricking
> Pyperclip into running them with whatever permissions the Python process has.

So, I just said to myself: "forget pyperclip, there should be a safer way to do it" but ,there are many password managers that use pyperclip, for example [here](https://github.com/gabfl/vault/blob/master/src/lib/Vault.py) and [here](https://github.com/mukkachaitanya/Password-Manager); anyway, I used tkinter's clipboard functions, but I'm facing with some problems; so I decided to use pyperclip. But I'm not sure about that security note. Is it safe to use pyperclip in password manager(or any other programs like this working with secrets and passwords)?

thank's for reading my question :)

issue_title: Load both the X clipboard *and* the GTK one.
issue_body:
Hi,

I see that pyperclip will load the first clipboard it can using whichever tool it can. The code looks like this:

```
    # Set one of the copy & paste functions.
    if xclipExists:
        _functions = 'xclip command' # for debugging
        paste = _pasteXclip
        copy = _copyXclip
    elif gtkInstalled:
        _functions = 'gtk module' # for debugging
        paste = _pasteGtk
        copy = _copyGtk
    elif ...
```

This means, on a posix system, we load either the X clipboard (a.k.a. the PRIMARY clipboard ), _or_ the GTK one (a.k.a. the CLIPBOARD clipboard).

I would like to load _both_ of these clipboards.

I suppose we would need `_copyGtkAndXclip` and a `pasteGtkAndXclip` functions.

issue_title: Make COMP_TYPE available to completion functions
issue_body:

issue_title: overlapping trades and order/position
issue_body:
I just noticed a small problem with overlapping trades. Specifically, if trade 0 currently has an open long position with size 1, and then trade 1 started a sell order with size 1 and the order got executed. A while later, the trade 1 sent out another buy order to close the short position and the order got executed. Note that the trade 0 still remains open with long position. Internally this buy order was labeled as "opened". This is because positions from all three orders were aggregated together, and the net position is long with size 1. But then in strategy.py, from line 382 to line 390, even though the trade 1 is actually closed, the current code failed to add it to the pending trade list, so the notify_trade() function will not be called. It seems the PnL calculation doesn't get affected by this small problem. I tried to add the following two lines of code at line 389 right after the trade.update() call:
                if trade.isclosed:
                    self._tradespending.append(trade)

It seems to fix it. I just wonder whether you see any potential problems with this change.

issue_title: Wrong prefix for algorithm check when public key is present
issue_body:
hey,

took me a while to figure out but you're checking the wrong prefix when using an asymetric ES algorithm. According to [PyJWT documentation](https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/a1205186d7a4f9689b6b2efcd93bae89324a5841/docs/algorithms.rst), the prefix is `ES` and not [`EC`](https://github.com/wichert/pyramid_jwt/blob/69f51ae9a4e17a647e1a91cd0123efa79c46512f/src/pyramid_jwt/__init__.py#L17).
For me, this resulted in not loading the public key (being None).

Should be an easy fix for you.
Thx

issue_title: CRLF and suds fields on linux
issue_body:
The Autotask API is windows.  It's expecting CRLF.  Suds on linux gives just LF.

CRLF counts as two characters in the API field length limits.  
The webservice does handle submitting just LF, but it replaces LF with CRLF.

Change the suds text objects to use CRLF so the counts are right.
It also means submitting an entity with line separation in a text field will not show as changed anymore.

issue_title: Exporting div to xlsx
rpmlint reports scripts are packaged under python modules directories
issue_body:
rpmlint (a tool for checking RPM packaging style) uses shebang to decide whether a python file is a script.

https://github.com/jasonish/py-idstools/tree/1dccb619555097cbbddb60095fd8f7da385c722a/idstools/scripts packages the following files as non-executable scripts under the python modules directory:

idstools/scripts/u2fast.py
idstools/scripts/u2spewfoo.py
idstools/scripts/u2eve.py
idstools/scripts/u2json.py
idstools/scripts/eve2pcap.py
idstools/scripts/rulecat.py
idstools/scripts/gensidmsgmap.py

The recommended handling, as reported by rpmlint is to remove the shebang "#! /usr/bin/env python" from those files, like in the case of idstools/scripts/dumpdynamicrules.py

issue_title: clang2py can't handle incomplete structures.
issue_body:
clang2py doesn't generate code for incomplete C structures.

Considering next header file:
```
#ifndef T_H
#define T_H

struct Foo;
void do_something(struct Foo* foo);

#endif
```

clang2py issues a few warnings and doesn't generate definitions for ``struct Foo`` and ```do_something```

```
clang2py -o t.py t.h 
ERROR:cursorhandler:Structure struct_Foo is Incomplete t.h:5 align:-2 size:-2
ERROR:cursorhandler:Structure struct_Foo is Incomplete t.h:5 align:-2 size:-2
```

Generated t.py is empty:
```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
#
# TARGET arch is: []
# WORD_SIZE is: 8
# POINTER_SIZE is: 8
# LONGDOUBLE_SIZE is: 16
#
import ctypes




__all__ = \
    []
```

Having declared, but not defined structures is most common way in C to hide library internals and it's widely used.

issue_title: Support time period indicators
issue_body:
### Request:
Support the time period indicators that may be passed by `AMAZON.TIME`[1] in `convert.to_time`[2].
- night: `NI`,
- morning `MI`,
- afternoon `AF`,
- evening `EV`

**Possible ranges**[3]**:**
night:
{t | 21:00 <= t <= 00:00} U {t | 00:00 <= t < 5:00}

morning:
{t | 05:00 <= t < 12:00}

afternoon: 
{t | 12:00 <= t < 17:00}

evening: 
{t | 17:00 <= t < 21:00} 


[1]: [Slot type reference, AMAZON.TIME](https://developer.amazon.com/public/solutions/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/docs/built-in-intent-ref/slot-type-reference#time)
[2]: [Flask-ask convert.py](https://github.com/johnwheeler/flask-ask/blob/master/flask_ask/convert.py).
[3]: [Parts of the Day: Early morning, late morning, etc.](http://learnersdictionary.com/qa/parts-of-the-day-early-morning-late-morning-etc) @ Merriam-Webster

issue_title: EMAIL_BACKEND ='post_office.EmailBackend' necessary?
issue_body:
Is it absolutely necessary to have `EMAIL_BACKEND ='post_office.EmailBackend'` ?

I think this in general is not desirable. In a production environment you want to be notified as soon as possible of exceptions or problems with the site itself, so having important emails with errors going to the database is undesirable. This would also be a problem if post_office itself developed a problem, or even the database, which prevented all emails from being sent.

So I would argue it is a much safer configuration to keep the default email backend and have selected apps send their email to post_office directly.

issue_title: Seg fault on ubuntu 14.04 (3.13 kernel) not on 16.04 (4.4.0 kernel)
issue_body:
I'm seeing something that appears to be a kernel interaction with python-duckling - here are the steps to illustrate/reproduce:

Spinning up a (Digital Ocean defined) default ubuntu 14.04.5 x64 (3.13.0-121 kernel) image on 4Gig ram, two core Digital Ocean "droplet", and installing Ducking with the following:
```
apt-get update ; apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential git-core default-jre python-pip python-dev python-setuptools ; pip install duckling
```

### On ubuntu 14.04 (3.13 kernel)
Duckling instance that **Segmentation Faults**:
```
# python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Oct 26 2016, 20:30:19) 
[GCC 4.8.4] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import duckling
>>> d= duckling.DucklingWrapper()
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
#
```

### On ubuntu 16.04 (4.4.0 kernel)
Repeating the same on Digital Ocean Ubuntu 16.04.2 x64 creates an instance that **works**:
```
# python
Python 2.7.12 (default, Nov 19 2016, 06:48:10) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

[{u'dim': u'time', u'end': 21, u'start': 11, u'value': {u'grain': u'minute', u'value': u'2017-07-26T11:45:00.000Z', u'others': [{u'grain': u'minute', u'value': u'2017-07-26T11:45:00.000Z'}, {u'grain': u'minute', u'value': u'2017-07-27T11:45:00.000Z'}, {u'grain': u'minute', u'value': u'2017-07-28T11:45:00.000Z'}]}, u'text': u'at 11:45am'}]
>>> 
```
(`d= duckling.DucklingWrapper()` took about 30 secs to return)


### On ubuntu 14.04 force loaded to have 4.4.0 kernel
Retrying on 14.04 but updating kernel (4.4.0-87) also **works**: 
( kernel update cmd from https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Kernel/LTSEnablementStack#Server-1 )
```
# apt-get install --install-recommends linux-generic-lts-xenial
[...bunch of output...]
# reboot

"not found" "san silvestro"
"not found" "notte di san silvestro"

(`d= duckling.DucklingWrapper()` took about 30 secs to return)
(while the results seem correct, the object instantiation seems to be triggering issue https://github.com/wit-ai/duckling_old/issues/210)

```python
import codecs

issue_title: Define a minimum version of "requests" to mitigate issue on Kali linux
Reverse doesn't work
issue_body:
Let's say our queryset looks like is so:
`qs = MyModel.objects.all()`

`list(qs.iter_smart())` returns non-empty list
`list(qs.reverse().iter_smart())` returns empty list `[]`

The bug is caused by a `max_pk` and `min_pk` assuming `min_pk <= max_pk` which isn't the case for `reverse`.
https://github.com/adamchainz/django-mysql/blob/66788e709c7b5196a9bc3f97f17993f039be9e36/django_mysql/models/query.py#L173

issue_title: Reduce boilerplate with special field
issue_body:
The readme suggests this usage:
```
type = models.CharField(max_length=1,
                            choices=PersonType.choices,
                            validators=[PersonType.validator])
```

However, we could provide default behavior with a custom CharField:
* `choices` accepts `DjangoChoices` subclass
* `max_length` is inferred from max(GivenChoices.values, key=len)
* `validators` is `[GivenChoices.validator]` by default

(While most of these are a matter of preference, automatically picking the `max_length` value would surely be an useful feature)

Then we can simply do this
```
type = ChoiceField(choices=PersonType.choices)
```

```python
        exc_type, exc_val, tb = err
        tb = ''.join(traceback.format_exception(
            exc_type,
            exc_val if isinstance(exc_val, exc_type) else exc_type(exc_val),
            tb
        ))
```

In my case, I was using `pypyodbc` whose exception classes all take _two_ arguments.  Whenever it raised one of those, the whole nose suite would bomb with `TypeError: __init__() takes exactly 3 arguments (2 given)`

issue_title: Templatetag js_reverse_inline breaks on Django 1.9
issue_body:
I'm using the `{% js_reverse_inline %}` template tag to put the script on a couple of pages in a project. Those pages started failing with a JavaScript SyntaxError once the project was updated from Django 1.8 to 1.9, which looks to be because of [this change in template tag escaping](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/releases/1.9/#simple-tag-now-wraps-tag-output-in-conditional-escape). The output of the `simple_tag` is escaped by default now and a SyntaxError is raised when the parser hits `&&`.

You can work around the error by turning autoescape off around the tag:

```html
{% autoescape off %}
<script type="text/javascript" charset="utf-8">{% js_reverse_inline %}</script>
{% endautoescape %}
```

Looking at the release notes, the proper fix seems to be to wrap the return value of `js_reverse_inline` with `mark_safe`.

issue_title: Do not serve schema file from absolute path in config
issue_body:
This would be a breaking change so something for major release.

Basicly:

instead of doing something like:

    pyramid_swagger.schema_directory = schemas/live/here

We would do:

    pyramid_swagger.schema_location = my_app.module

This would be more in line with what pyramid uses, besides for applications that are installed via pip providing schema directory would be problematic.

To get actual absolute file location from schema definitio one could do then:

    schema_dir = pkg_resources.resource_filename('myapp', 'module')

`pkg_resources` also has tools for directly fetching the contents of schema as string if we want that.

issue_title: Consider a high-performance wrapper using the unity build
issue_body:
CRoaring has a unity build:
https://github.com/RoaringBitmap/CRoaring#amalgamationunity-build

This avoids the nagging problem of dealing with a generic static library. Instead, you have a single header file and a single ``.c`` file that can be included directly. 

Using such a unity build, it should be possible to produce a higher-performance wrapper with good inlining. Moreover, the unity build could be included in this repository, making it super easy for people to proceed.

issue_title: Make an abstract user class to implement
issue_body:
<!-- Have a question? Please ask it on https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flask-login?sort=votes -->

- [X] Ensure you are using the latest PyPI release.
- [X] Read the [CHANGES](https://github.com/maxcountryman/flask-login/blob/master/CHANGES) document thoroughly.
- [x] Provide a clear and simple set of steps to reproduce your issue for others.

## Feature request:
### Have a class `AbcUser` to inherit from, which will in turn document the methods that must be implemented from.
```py
from typing import Text #Text is the typing for unicode strings, see https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.Text
from abc import ABC,abstractmethod #see https://docs.python.org/3/library/abc.html
class AbcUser(ABC):
     @abstractmethod
     def get_id(self) ->Text:
        """This method must return a unicode that uniquely identifies this user,
and can be used to load the user from the user_loader callback. Note that this must be a unicode -
 if the ID is natively an int or some other type, you will need to convert it to unicode."""
        pass
```

this way we can introduce both typing and documentation inside the built into the code and have it loosely enforced  

----
This way

```py
class MyUser(AbcUser):
def my_method():
...    pass
# Doesn't implement get_id()

user = MyUser()
```
results in
```
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-12-baad56b7d4c5> in <module>()
      4 # Doesn't implement get_id()
      5
----> 6 user = MyUser()

TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class MyUser with abstract methods get_id
```

issue_title: Issue (and solutions) under OSX with Macports
issue_body:
To make pythran (0.7) work under OSX with macports I need to tune some parts:
First to install boots:
sudo port install boost +python27

By default macport use clang as default compiler and boost and all dependant libs are compiled for clang.

You have to specifiy where to find libboost_python-mt.dylib.

edit ~/.pythranrc:
[user]
cxx=clang++
ldflags= -I/opt/local/include -L/opt/local/lib

be sure to specify -L/opt/local/lib where libboost_python-mt is situated.
Next I had issue with complex:
edit /opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pythran/pythonic/types/traits.hpp
Replace:
namespace std {
template <class T> class complex;
}

with
#include <complex>

And finally to solve issue at linking time, where clang could not find x86_64 symbol, force use of clang ++ in .pythranrc and you should be Good to go.

issue_title: Vectorization not implemented yet on 2D numpy array broadcasting
issue_body:
Hi @serge-sans-paille,

Thanks for your work on pythran. 
As discussed with you in class, when implementing a 2D convolution, the code generated by pythran is not vectorized, although it could be. Minimum working example:
```
#file convolve.py
#pythran export convolve_mean2_pythran(float[][])
import numpy as np
def convolve_mean2_pythran(image):
    height, width = image.shape
    return 0.25*(image[:-2,:]+image [2:,:]+image[1:-1,:]+image[1:-1,:])
```
We compile with:
`pythran -g -DUSE_BOOST_SIMD convolve.py -march=native -O0`

And run this:
`python -c "import numpy as np; x=np.empty((20, 20)); import convolve; convolve.convolve_mean2_pythran(x)"`

By inspecting the generated code with you, we ended up here:
```
template <class Arg>
    numpy_iexpr<Arg> &numpy_iexpr<Arg>::operator=(numpy_iexpr<Arg> const &expr)
    {
      assert(buffer);
      return utils::broadcast_copy<numpy_iexpr &, numpy_iexpr const &, value,
                                   value - utils::dim_of<numpy_iexpr>::value,
                                   false /*NIY*/>(*this, expr);
    }
```

Hope you can get it to work! 

Best,
Florian

issue_title: Cannot setup `log_format` in `setup.cfg, because of `InterpolationMissingOptionError`
issue_body:
I would like to setup `log_format` in the project-wise configuration file `setup.cfg`. I do it like this:
```
[tool:pytest]
log_format = %(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(name)s%(levelname)s%(message)s
```

Given this, any invocation of some `setup.py` or simply `pip install -r requirements.txt` in the directory where `setup.cfg` resides, gives me a error:
```
configparser.InterpolationMissingOptionError: Bad value substitution: option 'log_format' in section 'tool:pytest' contains an interpolation key 'filename' which is not a valid option name. Raw value: '%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(name)s%(levelname)s%(message)s'
```

As far as I understand, the reason is that %-formatting is used in config files for string interpolation. It is recommended to screen `%` with another `%` to avoid interpolation. Well, if I do this then `pytest-catchlog` stops interpreting this string as format and simply output it as is.

It looks like, `pytest-catchlog` shall adopt the rules of writing `setup.cfg` files. If there is another solution or workaround, please let me know.

issue_title: Error when request is JSON but a list rather than an object
issue_body:
Hi,

We are hitting an issue when upgrading to version 3.0.0 with the following line of code:
https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/core.py#L231

Our application responds to JSON-RPC requests and it is perfectly acceptable to send batch requests, in which case the JSON request is actually an array of requests. This means `request.get_json()` returns a list rather than a dict, and so `data.get(args_key, token)` fails with `'list' object has no attribute 'get'`.

As a side note - from what I can see, it is not possible to bypass Flask-Security for certain requests (even if the view doesn't require auth, it still goes through the Flask-Security middleware to identify the user, etc). In our case we would like to use Flask-Security for our admin views but we use our own authentication for our API views. Is there any way we can bypass the middleware for our API views? If not, having some mechanism like what Flask-Principal does for static files would be useful: https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-principal/blob/0b5cd06b464ae8b60939857784bda86c03dda1eb/flask_principal.py#L471-L473

Thanks

issue_title: Renaming polymodel migrations in django 1.8
issue_body:
Hello!

i am using django-polymodels and I needed to rename a model which inherits from another model which inherits from PolymorphicModel.  I renamed the model and ran makemigrations which properly detected the rename and made RenameModel migrations. However, when executing this, I got a warning saying that the content type of my old name is stale and needs to be deleted. When answering 'yes' to that, all model instances are deleted by cascading.

As I am not using content types myself, I figured it is related to the models being a polymodel since django-polymodels uses content types. I tried to circumvent this in many ways, for example by creating a new model to copy over all data in a data migration and then deleting the old model, but I also ran into problems with polymodels in the django 1.8 migrations. When loading models like so:

CombinedProduct = apps.get_model("product", "CombinedProduct")

and creating instances like CombinedProduct.create(...) or CombinedProduct(), I get an error saying that content type id null constraint failed. So it seems impossible to create new instances of models based on polymodels through the method advised in migrations.

Are these issues just me, or are they issues in the polymodels library? If not, how do I do proper rename migrations with django-polymodels?

issue_title: Names of actions conflict with translations elsewhere
issue_body: I think the names of the actions (addition, deletion, change) should be marked for translation with context, to avoid them being incorrectly translated. Mainly the word “change” is problematic, because it is ambiguous in English (can be either a verb or a noun).

This is trying to pretty-print a [`patsy.infix_parser.ParseNode`](https://github.com/pydata/patsy/blob/924b387652d13f816fd4a32983aae2ccba38fd84/patsy/infix_parser.py#L75), whose [`__repr__`](https://github.com/pydata/patsy/blob/924b387652d13f816fd4a32983aae2ccba38fd84/patsy/infix_parser.py#L82) uses a helper [`repr_pretty_delegate`](https://github.com/pydata/patsy/blob/924b387652d13f816fd4a32983aae2ccba38fd84/patsy/util.py#L432-L433) which just calls `IPython.lib.pretty.pretty`, because I didn't want to reimplement `__repr__` twice, once normally and once using IPython's system. I guess the problem is that you're monkeypatching `IPython.lib.pretty.RepresentationPrinter.pretty`, so we end up in a loop where you don't recognize my object so you try to call `__repr__`, then my `__repr__` tries to call IPython's `pretty`, which goes back into your code and doesn't recognize my object so you try to call `__repr__`...

(BTW, you may also appreciate [`repr_pretty_impl`](https://github.com/pydata/patsy/blob/924b387652d13f816fd4a32983aae2ccba38fd84/patsy/util.py#L437-L454), which is exactly the same interface as your `pretty_call`, but implemented using IPython's pretty-printer and about 5 years ago. And in case you're curious, here's what IPython prints for that code if you don't have prettyprinter enabled:
<details>

```
In [2]: patsy.parse_formula.parse_formula("y ~ x1 + x2")
Out[2]: 
ParseNode('~',
          Token('~', <Origin y ->~<- x1 + x2 (2-3)>),
          [ParseNode('PYTHON_EXPR',
                     Token('PYTHON_EXPR',
                           <Origin ->y<- ~ x1 + x2 (0-1)>,
                           extra='y'),
                     []),
           ParseNode('+',
                     Token('+', <Origin y ~ x1 ->+<- x2 (7-8)>),
                     [ParseNode('PYTHON_EXPR',
                                Token('PYTHON_EXPR',
                                      <Origin y ~ ->x1<- + x2 (4-6)>,
                                      extra='x1'),
                                []),
                      ParseNode('PYTHON_EXPR',
                                Token('PYTHON_EXPR',
                                      <Origin y ~ x1 + ->x2<- (9-11)>,
                                      extra='x2'),
                                [])])])
```
</details>
So you can see why I'm interested in this... :-))

Any ideas on how to avoid this? I suppose one obvious solution would be to go through and register `prettyprinter` printers for all my objects. It would be nice if `prettyprinter` could do something more out-of-the-box compatible here, though, so to improve the possibilities of it becoming a standard thing in IPython -- maybe if an object has a `_repr_pretty_` it could dispatch back into the real un-monkeypatched IPython formatting system?

Also, is there any way to do something like this `__repr__` → `prettyprinter` trick to avoid implementing all my reprs multiple times?

issue_title: Action Space
issue_body:
In hindsight, the action space seems to be missing some potentially necessary actions. Should this be revisited? One alternative is to avoid the discrete action space entirely and define the action space as a one-hot vector over UDLRAB. This allows all potential button combinations; however, increases the search space complexity.

```python
self.actions = [
    'U',   # Up
    'D',   # Down
    'L',   # Left
    'R',   # Right
    'UR',  # Up + Right
    'DR',  # Down + Right
    'URA', # Up + Right + A
    'DRB', # Down + Right + B
    'A',   # A
    'B',   # B
    'RB',  # Right + B
    'RA'   # Right + A
]
```

@stefankoegl - thanks for all of your work. 

Feature request - 

I am manually constructing a patch from a set of pre-created jsonpointer objects.  When I create the patch I'm using the `path` property of the pointer:

issue_title: Changes proposal
issue_body:
Hi there!

I found your module quite interesting and relevant for our needs. However, for our use case, I would propose a couple of updates/changes:
1. extending the model to include also additional info field, which would contain the message about the change (e.g. could be used as a summary of what is being changed and why). The point here to help users to digest the new terms. 
2. adding a template tag which would allow to display a modal with new terms, if such exists. This can be yet another means to communicate the new terms for users (e.g. by including the tag in the base template), an alternative to redirecting

If you accept the proposal, I could work towards a respective pull request. Alternatively, if you don't approve, I'm okey with introducing these changes in a separate repo. Let me know.
In any case, thanks a lot for your work and efforts! ;)

Regards,
Adrian

issue_title: Wrong bootstrap column calculations
issue_body:
As per object, on Bootstrap's *col-xs*-sized devices (expecially when running Chrome) there's a small overlap between the two big main divs, *#content* and *#sidebar-menu*. This is preventing both correct website usage and sidebar menu hiding (the *.show-hide-sidebar-menu* button falls behind the sidebar-menu div and hence it's not visible).

I've noticed this is due to a wrong match between the number of columns assigned to sidebar div and the number of columns assigned to the content div when dealing with *col-xs*-sized devices. Here's the code:

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:65**
`<div id="sidebar-menu" class="col-xs-6 col-sm-3 col-md-2 sidebar-menu">`

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:80**
`<div id="content" class="{% if is_displaying_menu %}col-xs-9` [...]

As you can see, with col-xs-6 and col-xs-9 we're having a total of 15 columns here... A bit too many :)

I've fixed that by replacing the "9" with another "6", which as far as I have seen leads to the best result. Any combination with a total of 12 would of course work as well. In any case, we have to accordingly replace as well the value here, or the menu won't close correcly:

**bootstrap_admin/static/bootstrap_admin/js/base.js:31**
`main_classes = 'col-xs-9 ` [...]

In my case, I've put a "6" there as well, as it has to match the one in *base.html:83*.

Hope this helps.
Cheers.

API response does not contain field `user_id`, used by drfso2 by default. I made simple workaround for this issue in my settings.py

```python
# monkeypathing social vk api
from social.backends.vk import VKOAuth2
VKOAuth2.ID_KEY = 'uid'
```

issue_title: Missing access_token in extra_data
issue_body:
The `user social auths` model created by `python-social-auth` has a json blog called `extra_data`. It contains info like the `username` and `access_token` from the provider.
I'm using the github backend and previously had my own endpoint to authenticate the user myself and take action when provided an access token from github. In that scenario, `extra_data` would contain the `access_token` which could be used for making API calls to github from the backend.

I have now switched to django-rest-framework-social-oauth2 and have noticed that the `access_token` is now null. `id` and `login` are still there and correct.

issue_title: Cannot access all search parameters from client.py

issue_title: Approval Tests with pypy
issue_body:
I have [pypy](https://github.com/squeaky-pl/portable-pypy#latest-python-35-release) installed and I would like to run approval tests with pypy. When I have simple example like this: 
```
import unittest

from approvaltests.approvals import verify
from approvaltests.reporters.generic_diff_reporter_factory import GenericDiffReporterFactory


class DummyTest(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_dummy(self):
        factory = GenericDiffReporterFactory()
        reporter = factory.get_first_working()
        verify('foobar', reporter)


if __name__ == "__main__":
        unittest.main()
```
And when I run it like this: `pypy3.5 dummy_test.py`, then I get error like this: 
```

mv -f /home/username/apps/pypy3.5-6.0.0-linux_x86_64-portable/lib-python/3/unittest/DummyTest.__call__.received.txt /home/username/apps/pypy3.5-6.0.0-linux_x86_64-portable/lib-python/3/unittest/DummyTest.__call__.approved.txt


E
======================================================================
ERROR: test_dummy (__main__.DummyTest)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "utest/test/keywords/test_dummy.py", line 12, in test_dummy
    verify('foobar', reporter)
  File "/home/godtdd/apps/pypy3.5-6.0.0-linux_x86_64-portable/site-packages/approvaltests/approvals.py", line 35, in verify
    verify_with_namer(data, get_default_namer(), reporter)
  File "/home/godtdd/apps/pypy3.5-6.0.0-linux_x86_64-portable/site-packages/approvaltests/approvals.py", line 44, in verify_with_namer
    raise ApprovalException(error)
approvaltests.approval_exception.ApprovalException: 'Approval Mismatch'

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.057s

``` 
It looks like that in pypy the namer.py [get_base_name](https://github.com/approvals/ApprovalTests.Python/blob/master/approvaltests/core/namer.py#L27) does not work correctly and it returns something that refers to unitest framework installed in pypy. In my case it is something like this: `'/home/username/apps/pypy3.5-6.0.0-linux_x86_64-portable/lib-python/3/unittest/DummyTest.__call__'` When I run the same test with Python 2.7 or 3.6 the `get_base_name` works correctly and returns in my case this `u'/home/username/workspace/SeleniumLibrary/utest/test/keywords/approved_files/DummyTest.test_dummy'`. 

By debugging issue little further, it looks like [is_test_method](https://github.com/approvals/ApprovalTests.Python/blob/master/approvaltests/core/namer.py#L67) does not work correctly with pypy and it returns `True` also for pypy unitest frames. I was wondering could it be fixed in the [get_test_frame](https://github.com/approvals/ApprovalTests.Python/blob/master/approvaltests/core/namer.py#L59) method. It currently looks like this: 
``` 
    def get_test_frame(self, caller):
        frameNumber = 1
        for index, frame in enumerate(caller):
            if self.is_test_method(frame):
                frameNumber = index

        return frameNumber
``` 
and could it be changed like this:
```
    def get_test_frame(self, caller):
        frameNumber = 1
        for index, frame in enumerate(caller):
            if self.is_test_method(frame):
                return index
        raise AssertionError('Test frame not found.')
```
At least it fixes my problem, but my knowledge on ApprovalTests is limited and I do not know is there a good reason to loop trough all frames.

issue_title: l2_normalization for fp16 got 0.0 when data is very large
issue_body:
If the data is set as follow:
in_data0 = mx.nd.random.uniform(-5, 5, (512,100000), ctx=mx.gpu(0))

mx.symbol.L2Normalization will got 0.0, whether forward or backward.
But in mx.symbol.norm, it is OK.
Test code as follow:

> import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet.test_utils import default_context, assert_almost_equal, check_numeric_gradient
import time
in_data0 = mx.nd.random.uniform(-5, 5, (512,100000), ctx=mx.gpu(0))
def check_l2_normalizationFP16(mode, dtype, norm_eps=1e-10, isfp16=False):
    ctx = mx.gpu(0)
    data = mx.symbol.Variable('data', dtype=dtype)
    out = mx.symbol.L2Normalization(data=data, mode=mode, eps=norm_eps)
    out = mx.sym.make_loss(out)
    in_data = in_data0.astype(dtype)
    a=time.time()
    exe = out.simple_bind(ctx=ctx, data=in_data.shape, dtype=dtype)
    output = exe.forward(is_train=True, data=in_data)
    exe.backward()
    symbolic_grads = exe.grad_dict['data'].asnumpy()
    print('forw---',in_data.dtype,output[0].dtype,'--'+mode,'--')
    print('grad---',in_data.dtype,symbolic_grads[0].dtype,'--'+mode,'--',100*(time.time()-a))
    return output,symbolic_grads

issue_title: How to use gluon to train GAN network on multiple GPUs?
issue_body:
I come up with a solution, but it trains discriminator for an epoch and then uses the same thing to train the generator.
```
def forward_backward(net, data, label):
    with autograd.record():
        losses_D = [loss(netD(X), Y) for X, Y in zip(data, label)]
    for l in losses_D:
        l.backward()

    with autograd.record():
        losses_G = [loss(netG(X), Y) for X, Y in zip(data, label)]
    for l in losses_G:
        l.backward()

label = gluon.utils.split_and_load(trainging_data, ctx)
forward_backward(net, data, label)
```
I want to update the both network by batch.  Something like:
```
def forward_backward(net, data, label):
    for X, Y in zip(data, label)
        with autograd.record():
            losses_D.append(loss(netD(X), Y))

        for l in losses_D:
            l.backward()

        with autograd.record():
            losses_G.append(loss(netG(X), Y))

        for l in losses_G:
            l.backward()

label = gluon.utils.split_and_load(trainging_data, ctx)
forward_backward(net, data, label)
```

I have no idea if this code will work. Please let me know if it works.

issue_title: Passing data from numpy trough the intermediate API and getting back the cross entropy
issue_body:
There are quite a few questions which are unclear of how to do. First, I want to initalize the model without any data given/binded. This seems quite difficult and could not find an example.

Secondly, I want to be able to pass numpy data to the forward and backward methods of the intermediate API, rather than using the `fit` method, or alternatively is there anyway to get a sensible callback which to return the cross entorpy + the time taken for the iteration?

My code currently looks like this:
```
    x = mx.symbol.Variable("inputs")
    y = mx.symbol.Variable("targets")

    h = x
    tanh = net.nonlinearity_from_string("tanh")
    softmax = net.nonlinearity_from_string("softmax")
    for i in range(1, len(arch_specs)-1):
        h = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(h, name="ff_" + str(i), num_hidden=arch_specs[i])
        h = tanh(h)
    h = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(h, name="ff_" + str(len(arch_specs)-1), num_hidden=arch_specs[-1])
    h = softmax(h, y)

    mod = mx.mod.Module(h, data_names=["inputs"], label_names=["targets"])
    mod.init_optimizer(optimizer_params={'learning_rate': learning_rate})
    metric = mx.metric.create('ce')

    for i in range(iterations):
        i1 = (i * batch_size) % num_samples
        # print(i1, i2)
        start_time = time.time()
        mod.forward(x[i1:i1 + batch_size])
        mod.update_metric(metric, y[i1:i1+batch_size])
        mod.backward()
        mod.update()
        train_time[i] = time.time() - start_time
        loss[i] = metric.get_name_value()["acc"]
        if debug:
            print("Train [{}]: {:.2f} - {:.3e}".format(i, train_time[i], loss[i]))
```

I'm not totally sure if this is the correct way, however atm on the line for creating the optimizer I get:
```
File "/home/alex/work/python/deep-bench/deep_bench/frameworks/mxnet/ff.py", line 19, in build_ff_net
    mod.init_optimizer(optimizer_params={'learning_rate': learning_rate})
  File "/home/alex/work/python/mxnet/python/mxnet/module/module.py", line 452, in init_optimizer
    assert self.binded and self.params_initialized
AssertionError
```

The main goal is to use this in a benchmark facility so any suggestions of how to do this properly in MXNet are welcome.
