store: Automatically remove nodes from Ceph on autoscale or IP change
issue_body:
Ceph expects nodes to have constant IP addresses. When nodes are added to the cluster, there's no problem - the Ceph services start and join the cluster. When nodes are removed (such as on an auto-descale), however, Ceph sees the host as down. This potentially becomes a problem once there are a lot of dead nodes that haven't been properly removed from Ceph, potentially resulting in Ceph not having enough healthy monitors to achieve a cluster quorum.

Additionally, when AWS relocates an instance to a new host machine, its IP is likely to change. When the store containers come up on the new IP address, they see themselves as new and Ceph creates new host entries. The old IP address becomes a stale entry in Ceph which will never come up again.

Removing entries from the cluster is done quite easily by following the [Adding/Removing Hosts](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/add_remove_host/). However, this process is manual, and oftentimes I imagine users are unaware that their nodes are autoscaling or being relocated. We should try to find a way to detect these changes and automatically remove old nodes from the cluster.

issue_title: Proposal feat(controller): add fine-grain scheduling policy
issue_body:
The idea behind this is the creation of a default policy to schedule apps inside the cluster using only the patterns shared by different implementations in fleet (the only option for now), mesos (and kubernetes?).
It must be possible to define a default configuration for all the apps but if any app can have a custom policy that will override the default.

The idea of this proposal is to see if this makes sense to other users and what other options to schedule can be added


Policies:
- one instance per node
- metadata


proposed etcd content:
```
/deis/controller/schedulingPolicy/default         -> [onePerNode | metadata]
/deis/controller/schedulingPolicy/apps/<app name> -> [onePerNode | metadata]
```


**Only one instance per node:**

mesos:
```
{
 "instances": XX,
 "constraints": [
   ["hostname", "UNIQUE"],
   ["<app name_type.vXXX>", "GROUP_BY"]
 ]
}
```

fleet:
```
{
  "section": "X-Fleet",
  "name": "Conflicts",
  "value": "<app name_type.vXXX>"
}
```


**Schedule using metadata:**

mesos:
```
{
  "instances": XX,
  "constraints": [
    ["rack", "CLUSTER", "rack-1"]
  ]
}
```

fleet:
```
{
  "section": "X-Fleet",
  "name": "MachineMetadata",
  "value": "rack=rack-1"
}
```

https://github.com/coreos/fleet/blob/master/Documentation/unit-files-and-scheduling.md
https://github.com/mesosphere/marathon/blob/master/docs/docs/constraints.md#unique-operator
https://github.com/mesosphere/marathon/blob/master/docs/docs/constraints.md#group_by-operator
https://github.com/mesosphere/marathon/blob/master/docs/docs/constraints.md#cluster-operator

issue_title: Several Endpoint types can't autoactivate, may be a problem for hosted CLI transition
issue_body:
There's at least a couple of types of activation other than S3 endpoints which can be done via the hosted CLI, but which we can't replicate.

GSI-SSH credentials aren't going to be supported.
Do we have any fallback for endpoints which may still be using `endpoint-activate -g`?
If not, the hosted CLI may need to drop `endpoint-activate -g` support prior to the transition.

MyProxy-activation can be done directly from the CLI.
At the very least, there's the possibility of someone scripting with `echo "..." | ssh ... endpoint-activate ...`
It also seems that there may be scenarios in which the MyProxy server may issue a fresh proxy cert without requiring username/password? I have one user story that suggests this to be the case, but cannot verify at present.

For MyProxy endpoints, should we consider reintroducing `globus endpoint activate`, consuming myproxy credentials?

cc @ranantha

issue_title: Add support for dependency tracking
issue_body:
To help diagnose server performance issues, it's be great to add support for Application Insight's dependency tracking feature

General:
* Fix contract for Remote Dependency Data
* Add track dependency method to the telemetry client
 
Django Middleware:
* Set the parent ID for the request being processed (see #55) 
* Expose the request ID somehow so that clients can set the parent ID appropriately when calling track dependency. One way to do this is to just expose a telemetry client using thread local storage with the parent ID set to the request ID. (However, I don't think using TLS is recommended by the Django team.)

These changes are all prototyped here: https://github.com/twschiller/ApplicationInsights-Python/commit/fdff3d8fca64597fd2b3e408ed8a2af77d66ce02. 

For those wondering how to track their SQL/cache dependencies when using Django:
* SQL: wrap the `CursorWrapper.execute` and `CursorWrapper.executemany` methods
* Redis (using `django_redis`): wrap the `RedisCache.get` method, which includes both the network call and the deserialization

issue_title: Context properties of custom Channel ignored in LoggingHandler
issue_body:
I'm trying to set up a LoggingHandler with a few context properties but this is impossible currently:
```py
import logging
from applicationinsights import channel
from applicationinsights.logging import LoggingHandler

channel = channel.TelemetryChannel()
channel.context.properties['foo'] = 'bar'
handler = LoggingHandler(key, telemetry_channel=channel)
logging.basicConfig(handlers=[handler], format='%(message)s', level=logging.DEBUG)

logging.info("foo") # trace does not include foo property
logging.shutdown()
```

The reason this is not working is that LoggingHandler constructs a new TelemetryClient instance which then ignores the existing context from the passed channel object:
```py
class TelemetryClient(object):
    def __init__(self, instrumentation_key, telemetry_channel=None):
        ...
        self._context = channel.TelemetryContext()
        self._context.instrumentation_key = instrumentation_key
        self._channel = telemetry_channel or channel.TelemetryChannel()

    @property
    def context(self):
        return self._context
```

Shouldn't this be instead:
```py
class TelemetryClient(object):
    def __init__(self, instrumentation_key, telemetry_channel=None):
        self._channel = telemetry_channel or channel.TelemetryChannel()
        self.context.instrumentation_key = instrumentation_key

    @property
    def context(self):
        return self._channel.context
```
?

issue_title: Docs error - module_functions_template.rst
issue_body:
h2. Docs error - module_functions_template.rst

In v0.10.1 the autodoc in Sphinx works in module_functions_template.rst - http://tsfresh.readthedocs.io/en/v0.10.1/text/list_of_features.html
In v0.11.0 it broken with and does not exist on readthedocs 404s - http://tsfresh.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11.0/text/list_of_features.html

But weirdly it is there with the error on latest and on v0.10.1 on stable, weird.

Anyway on latest it has thrown.

```
System Message: WARNING/2 (text/_generated/tsfresh.feature_extraction.feature_calculators.rst, line 5)
failed to import mean_second_derivate_central
```

This is undoubtedly related to the change in Version 0.11.0

* renamed mean_second_derivate_central to mean_second_derivative_central

Done in https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/issues/324 and https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/pull/334/commits/3926ce7c9b2cf4026286c7334b3267353fabc1de

However I cannot find any reason why that should have thrown that error, as I
assume the build on readthedocs was done on a checkout of that version 
automatically so, if so it should have had those changes, and the change itself
is trivial really, although you know my feelings on immutable things ;)

Interestingly though, ```mean_second_derivative_central``` is there in the latest ***but*** it is the only one that does not have it's docstring in the other column.
Other functions with ```math``` functions in there docstrings have their docstrings in the column.

issue_title: Trinity seems to hit a deadlock when terminating its sub-processes
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

When trinity is shutting down, it sends a SIGINT to its subprocesses and calls `.join()` to wait (up to 5s) for them. However, the join() seems to always timeout, at which point it sends a SIGTERM and calls `.join()` again, but at this time the join seems to return immediately.

This seems to be a known caveat when using multiprocessing.Queue (which all trinity subprocesses use for logging) and attempting to `.join()`:

> This means that whenever you use a queue you need to make sure that all items which have been put on the queue will eventually be removed before the process is joined. Otherwise you cannot be sure that processes which have put items on the queue will terminate. Remember also that non-daemonic processes will be joined automatically.

(from https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/multiprocessing.html?highlight=process#multiprocessing-programming)

### How can it be fixed

Need to investigate how to properly fix it

issue_title: `cytoolz` version conflict after update to Populus 2.2.0
issue_body:
* OS: Arch Linux (last full update 2017-12-18)
* Python: 3.6.3
* `pip`: 9.0.1
* Populus: 2.2.0

### What's wrong?

Upgrading to Populus 2.2.0 (from 2.1.0) breaks package dependencies.

Post-upgrade in an existing Solidity `Greeter` project, running `pytest` fails with:

``` python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages/_pytest/vendored_packages/pluggy.py", line 510, in load_setuptools_entrypoints
    plugin = ep.load()
  File "/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2404, in load
    self.require(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2427, in require
    items = working_set.resolve(reqs, env, installer, extras=self.extras)
  File "/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 875, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist, req).with_context(dependent_req)
pkg_resources.ContextualVersionConflict: (cytoolz 0.9.0 (/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages), Requirement.parse('cytoolz==0.8.2'), {'eth-tester'})

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:
<yada, more of the same>
_pytest.vendored_packages.pluggy.PluginValidationError: Plugin 'ethereum' could not be loaded: (cytoolz 0.9.0 (/mnt/data/veox/src/populus-greeter/.virtualenv/greeter/lib/python3.6/site-packages), Requirement.parse('cytoolz==0.8.2'), {'eth-tester'})!
```

That is, `eth-tester` [specifies](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-tester/blob/6f4c67c400aea54ad2d012ec92ec9f8e8a729a45/setup.py#L20) a `cytoolz==0.8.2` dependency, but `0.9.0` gets installed instead.

This is strange: I'd expect `pip` to detect the fact, and either install `cytoolz==0.8.2`, or, if something else requires `cytoolz>=0.9.0`, then warn the user.

### Workaround

After installing Populus, roll back the `cytoolz` dependency:

``` sh
pip install cytoolz==0.8.2
```

After this, issue #414 should be experienced on running `pytest`.

issue_title: Add logging messages to Providers
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Inspired from: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/issues/852#issuecomment-390247197

There are places where it would be useful for a user to enable logging on the `web3` namespace to get a better picture of what is happening.

### How can it be fixed?

A good starting point would be our providers.  Each provider should output some `DEBUG` level logging.

- `web3.manager.RequestManager`
  - in `_make_request` prior to issuing the request to the provider with `provider/rpc-method-name/json-serialized-params`
- `web3.providers.rpc.HTTPProvider`
  - prior to making the http request with `uri/rpc-method-name/json-serialized-params`
  - after getting http response `uri/xrpc-method-name/status-code`
- `web3.providers.ipc.IPCProvider`
  - same as `HTTPProvider` but with `ipc_path` instead of `uri`'
  - no response logging needed.
- `web3.providers.websocket.WebsocketProvider`
  - same as `HTTPProvider` but with the websocket `uri`
  - no response logging needed.

Other places I'm considering this but that should be done separately.

- At each call down the middleware stack which allows inspection of the path a call took through the middlewares, maybe into the provider, and back out of the middlewares.
- On `Web3` object construction outputing the enabled `modules` and `middlewares`

Specifics about loggers.

The loggers should be namespaced using the import path of the module level object they are embeded within..

- for classes, `logger = logging.getLogger('web3.providers.rpc.HTTPProvider')`
- for functions, `logger = logging.getLogger('web3.some_path.my_function')`

issue_title: Deprecate and remove `force_text` and `force_bytes` and dependent functions.
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `force_bytes` and `force_text` functions are bad.  They use `latin1` encoding due to the fact that `utf8` cannot encode all of the bytes values between 0-255.  This has caused much confusion given that `utf8` is the *default* encoding that most people expect.

### How can it be fixed?

Fully deprecate the *automatic* coercion of `text -> bytes` and `bytes -> text` including all of the functions which use the derivative functions like `coerce_return_to_*`, and `coerce_args_to_*`.

Introduce new functions `to_text`, `to_bytes` and maybe others, pulled from [Web3.py in `web3.utils.encoding`](https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/39ef04b30456d37bd5473bd9fecfd9def1c1e6f4/web3/utils/encoding.py#L123-L224).

Any function which previously dependend on the `coerce_args_to_*` should then be migrated to the new call format `fn(primative=None, hexstr=None, text=None)` format to remove implicit type conversions. 

Any function which depends on `coerce_return_to*` should be converted to do explicit conversions.

issue_title: Add ability to pin channel recursively
issue_body:
Moved from #7177 which is a different issue.

Add a flag `--recursive-channel-spec` which would apply a channel spec to not just the specified package but all of it's dependencies recursively - e.g.
```
conda install --recursive-channel-spec conda-forge::pyarrow
```

Would not only install pyarrow from `conda-forge` but would also install the conda-forge variant of all of it's dependencies (and their dependencies, and...)

This functionality would be complimentary to #3149 and would allow users to more easily avoid the current abi compatibility issues as demonstrated in https://github.com/conda-forge/pyarrow-feedstock/issues/5

issue_title: BUG: Cannot activate env on Windows with envs_dirs is set in .condarc
issue_body:
Using conda 4.0.1
Add envs_dirs to .condarc file on Windows.
Create a new env.
Do conda info -e to see the new env in the envs_dirs directory.
activate new env

The **activate will fail**.  It seems that activate doesn't support the envs_dirs config in .condarc.
The activate command is only looking in the default envs location on Windows.

issue_title: Suggestion: on failed installs due to permissions, check for  insufficient disk space
issue_body:
Hi Team Conda,

Great work on conda! I have a small issue to report: when you run out of disk space and try to install a package with conda it will tell you there is a permissions error. 

Might it be possible to distinguish incorrect permissions from insufficient diskspace? I'm not sure if it would be of general utility for a broader audience but I would gather this is going to happen time-to-time, especially if using conda on a machine with shared resources/quotas.

Thanks,
zach cp

* Linux variant and version (CentOS, RHEL, SUSE, etc.)

issue_title: Conda 4.2.10, 4.2.11 error when parsing .condarc with S3 channel URL
issue_body:
We've recently run into issues using our S3 conda channel. We actually found this because of `boto` upgrading `conda` from installed, stable versions to latest (4.2.11). This forces us to pin all of our conda environments to conda 4.2.9 (described at bottom). I looked at #3774 but adding a key prefix to the end of the bucket URL has no effect (this would be unsuitable in any case, but wanted to see if the issue was related).

```
(root) vagrant-ubuntu-trusty-64>> conda --version
conda 4.2.11
```

```
(root) vagrant-ubuntu-trusty-64>> conda search test
An unexpected error has occurred.

    return conda_exception_handler(_main)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/exceptions.py", line 497, in conda_exception_handler
    print_unexpected_error_message(e)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/exceptions.py", line 443, in print_unexpected_error_message
    info_stdout, info_stderr = get_info()
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/exceptions.py", line 413, in get_info
    args.func(args, p)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main_info.py", line 225, in execute
    channels = list(prioritize_channels(channels).keys())
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 380, in prioritize_channels
    channel = Channel(chn)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 161, in __call__
    c = Channel.from_value(value)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 211, in from_value
    return Channel.from_url(value)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 196, in from_url
    return parse_conda_channel_url(url)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 132, in parse_conda_channel_url
    configured_token) = _read_channel_configuration(scheme, host, port, path)
  File "/home/vagrant/mc/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/models/channel.py", line 122, in _read_channel_configuration

```

~/.condarc:

```yml
channels:
  - defaults
  - s3://our-conda-channel-bucket
  - conda-forge
```

To work around this issue:

* Comment out lines with S3 channel URLs in .condarc
* `conda install conda=4.2.9`
* Add `conda ==4.2.9` to $CONDA_ENV/conda-meta/pinned (or else other packages will upgrade it)
* Uncomment lines with S3 channel URLs in .condarc

issue_title: Set the default CondaSession.verify attribute from the config setting
issue_body:
Currently, the .condarc config setting must be explicitly used when making a request with a CondaSession object.  This means that anyone who wants to use CondaSession must check the .condarc ssl_verify value and appropriately interpret it, etc.  (For example, see https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/47e300b0e2cd5aad1dfe18d26eada5995b058004/conda/fetch.py#L101)

I think it would be much cleaner for CondaSession itself to set the default verify value in its __init__: `self.verify = <code to get the .condarc ssl_verify setting`.  See https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/8b5e457b756b2ab4c02473f7a42c2e0201ecc7e9/requests/sessions.py#L314 to see that this is correct.

This change would mean that we don't need the verify argument here: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/47e300b0e2cd5aad1dfe18d26eada5995b058004/conda/fetch.py#L101, and would also solve this issue: https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/523

issue_title: Abort if not tty ?
issue_body:
long story short, in a Notebook, using the IPython kernel.

```
!conda unpgrade whatever
...
Proceed ([y]/n)?
```

And then... you need to interrupt kernel to send a KeyboardInterrupt, which is far from intuitive for many users. 
Could it be possible  if not tty or not interactive or any other sensible condition to fail/exit and print that user should use `-y` ? The point being to not leave the kernel blocked regardless of the solution.

issue_body:
_From @rubennj on December 17, 2016 21:31_

Conda says it cannot write and therefore it doesn't install/update anything.
This happens when Miniconda is installed for all users, however it works when is installed only for the current user.

Version: Miniconda3-4.2.12-Windows-x86_64.exe

issue_title: BUG: Cannot activate env on Windows with envs_dirs is set in .condarc
issue_body:
Using conda 4.0.1
Add envs_dirs to .condarc file on Windows.
Create a new env.
Do conda info -e to see the new env in the envs_dirs directory.
activate new env

The **activate will fail**.  It seems that activate doesn't support the envs_dirs config in .condarc.
The activate command is only looking in the default envs location on Windows.

issue_title: What is the purpose of AngleLimitRegisterController?
issue_body:
What is the purpose of the controller `AngleLimitRegisterController` that is created by the `BaseDxlController` when initializing a Poppy Creature?

Practically the angle limits are set in `check_motor_eprom_configuration `while doing the initialization from json file in `from_config` and after that they don't change. The controller just reads the values so I can't really see any value in the work that is doing.

The controller runs at 10Hz - which is quite often -  and in it's own thread - which in my case with 4 DXL IOs means I have 4 threads extra when running the robot. This adds unnecessary overhead on the controller loops, not that much because of the processor usage, but because they compete with the other threads over the IO port and interferes with the `PosSpeedLoadDxlController` that is indeed important. 

Can we just take it out from the `BaseDxlController` initialization?

def case(store, institute_obj, case_obj):
    """Preprocess a single case."""
    case_obj['individual_ids'] = []
    for individual in case_obj['individuals']:
        try:
            sex = int(individual.get('sex', 0))
        except ValueError as err:
            sex = 0
        individual['sex_human'] = SEX_MAP[sex]
        individual['phenotype_human'] = PHENOTYPE_MAP.get(individual['phenotype'])
        case_obj['individual_ids'].append(individual['individual_id'])

    case_obj['assignees'] = [store.user(user_email) for user_email in
                             case_obj.get('assignees', [])]
    suspects = [store.variant(variant_id) or variant_id for variant_id in
                case_obj.get('suspects', [])]
    causatives = [store.variant(variant_id) or variant_id for variant_id in
                  case_obj.get('causatives', [])]
```
When creating `suspects` and `causatives` above it says 
```python
store.variant(variant_id) or variant_id
```
This means that we either get a list with variants (dictionaries) or variant_ids (strings) or a mix of those.
The problem arises later in the code when the result is sent to the function `variants_filter_by_field` in
`scout/server/blueprints/variants/controllers.py`. That one looks like:

```python
def variants_filter_by_field(store, variants, field, case_obj, institute_obj):
    """Given a list of variant objects return only those that have a key specified
      ....
    """
    filtered_variants = []
    # Check if the variants have information if "field"
    for var in variants:
        if var.get(field):
            # Add more details to the variant
            if var['category'] == 'snv':
                var_object = variant(store, institute_obj, case_obj, var['_id'])
            else:
                var_object = sv_variant(store, institute_obj['_id'], case_obj['display_name'], var['_id'])

            filtered_variants.append(var_object['variant'])

    return filtered_variants
```
It loops over `variants` and expects them to be dictionaries since it does `.get`. This fails when the objects are strings.

Could you fix?

issue_title: search method "default_operator" option raises an exception
reindex() should have a higher default scan size
issue_body:
While trying to re-index an ES 1.7 index in preparation for ES 2.X, we hit some pretty bad performance issues (see https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/18253 and https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/issues/397).  Turns out this was caused by `reindex()` using the default `size` kwarg for the `scan()` method, which is 10.  Obviously the optimal value depends on many factors, but 10 is much too small for a reindex operation.  For us, passing in a value of 1000 sped up our reindex by an order of magnitude:

```
es_helpers.reindex(
    args.sourceCluster,
    args.sourceIndex,
    args.targetIndex,
    target_client=args.workerTargetClient,
    chunk_size=args.chunkSize,
    scroll=args.scroll,
    scan_kwargs={'size': args.searchQuerySize},
    bulk_kwargs=bulkArgs)
```  

Note that elasticsearch-core's 2.3 reindex method just increased their default from 100 to 1000 (https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/18340).  I'm proposing that elasticsearch-py does the same.

It would be nice to back-port this to the 1.X branch too, in order to facilitate the migration of indices from 1.X to 2.X.  We would not have been able to upgrade to 2.X using elasticsearch-py unless we made this fix.

issue_title: how does parallel_bulk work?
issue_body:
python `3.4.1` elasticsearch-py `2.1.0`

I was having some trouble understanding how `parallel_bulk` works, I was trying to use it to index some data (10m records+) but ran into memory issues. I discovered that it uses multiprocessing, so (forked?) processes instead of threads, which I think explains the behavior I see, regardless I already wrote some memory benchmark that compares it to `streaming_bulk`, maybe we can develop a multithreaded bulk helper instead of the multiprocess one I think this would be useful. Or is there a way to use `parallel_bulk` without this excessive memory usage?

---

```python
import os
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch
from elasticsearch.helpers import streaming_bulk, parallel_bulk
import psutil
es = Elasticsearch()
try:
    es.indices.create(index='my_test')
except:
    pass

def test(bulk_fn, size):
    def generator():
        for num in range(size):
            yield {
                    '_index': 'my_test',
                    '_id': num,
                    '_type': 'document',
                    '_source': {'some_id': num, 'data': 'A'*1024},
                    }

    i = 0
    for ok, result in bulk_fn(es, generator()):
        if i % (size//5) == 0:
            process = psutil.Process(os.getpid())
            mem = process.memory_info().rss / float(2 ** 20)
            print('item: %d, memory: %.3f MiB' % (i, mem))
        i += 1
    print('----')

print('parallel_bulk (50000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 50000)
print('parallel_bulk (100000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 100000)
print('parallel_bulk (200000):'); test(parallel_bulk, 200000)
print('streaming_bulk (50000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 50000)
print('streaming_bulk (100000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 100000)
print('streaming_bulk (200000):'); test(streaming_bulk, 200000)

es.indices.delete(index='my_test')
```

issue_title: Elasticsearch 2.3: `error` attribute not available in bulk error object for delete action
Response bodies from failed requests should be logged
issue_body:
`log_request_success` current logs both the request and response bodies, while `log_request_fail` only logs the request body. An exception may be thrown, but there's nothing in the log beyond the HTTP status code.

This:

```
WARNING:elasticsearch:GET /blah/_search [status:404 request:0.012s]
DEBUG:elasticsearch:> {}
```

Should be:

```
WARNING:elasticsearch:GET /blah/_search [status:404 request:0.012s]
DEBUG:elasticsearch:> {}
DEBUG:elasticsearch:< {"error":{"root_cause":[{"type":"index_not_found_exception","reason":"no such index","resource.type":"index_or_alias","resource.id":"blah","index":"blah"}],"type":"index_not_found_exception","reason":"no such index","resource.type":"index_or_alias","resource.id":"blah","index":"blah"},"status":404}
```

I found that while using date math in index names, I couldn't actually see what the calculated index name was when it couldn't be found.

issue_title: [Help] Argument groups no longer display correctly
issue_body:
Likely a side effect of PR #832.

```
Command
    az storage blob delete: Marks the specified blob or snapshot for deletion.
        The blob is later deleted during garbage collection.

        Note that in order to delete a blob, you must delete all of its
        snapshots. You can delete both at the same time with the Delete
        Blob operation.

Arguments
    --container-name -c [Required]: The container name.
    --name -n           [Required]: The blob name.

Storage Account Arguments
    --account-key                 : Storage account key. Must be used in conjunction with storage
                                    account name. Environment variable: AZURE_STORAGE_KEY.
    --account-name                : Storage account name. Must be used in conjunction with either
                                    storage account key or a SAS token. Environment variable:
                                    AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT.
    --connection-string           : Storage account connection string. Environment variable:
                                    AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING.
    --delete-snapshots            : Required if the blob has associated snapshots.  Allowed values:
                                    include, only.
    --if-match                    : An ETag value, or the wildcard character (*). Specify this
                                    header to perform the operation only if the resource's ETag
                                    matches the value specified.
    --if-modified-since           : Alter only if modified since supplied UTC datetime
                                    (Y-m-d'T'H:M'Z').
    --if-none-match               : An ETag value, or the wildcard character (*). Specify this
                                    header to perform the operation only if the resource's ETag does
                                    not match the value specified. Specify the wildcard character
                                    (*) to perform the operation only if the resource does not
                                    exist, and fail the operation if it does exist.
    --if-unmodified-since         : Alter only if unmodified since supplied UTC datetime
                                    (Y-m-d'T'H:M'Z').
    --lease-id                    : Required if the blob has an active lease.

Storage Account Arguments
    --sas-token                   : A Shared Access Signature (SAS). Must be used in conjunction
                                    with storage account name. Environment variable:
                                    AZURE_STORAGE_SAS_TOKEN.
    --snapshot                    : The snapshot parameter is an opaque DateTime value that, when
                                    present, specifies the blob snapshot to delete.
    --timeout                     : Request timeout in seconds. Applies to each call to the service.
```

The arguments now appear in alphabetical order with potentially multiple group labels.

issue_title: More robust message receiving
issue_body:
It would be nice if message receiving was more robust. Specifically it would be nice if it was resilient to changes in the Service Bus implementation.

Currently _create_message() in _serialization.py is vulnerable to changes in the Service Bus, similar to what happened around 25.june 2016 when the 'strict-transport-security' header was added. This particular case was fixed with commits 97f97ce35dc92bdd5394d983a7b19ec84205898e and 073c97dfc5239dff9634432bb954397d832a7f7d, but the general weakness is still there: if any unexpected headers are received the Python SDK will fail to create a message. 

As a user of the SDK it is hard to remedy this without patching the SDK itself. It would be nice if messages could still be received in this situation, with the problem being logged instead of throwing an exception.

issue_title: azure.mgmt.scheduler.operations.JobsOperations.create_update() Malformed Job Object
azure-keyvault 1.0.0 doesn't work with MSIAuthentication
issue_body:
It looks like the issue is in KeyVaultAuthentication._init_ method:

```python
if not authorization_callback:
            def auth_callback(server, resource, scope, scheme):
                if self._credentials.resource != resource:
                    self._credentials.resource = resource
                    self._credentials.set_token()
```

with the code below, self._credentials.resource == resource, so _credentials.set_token() is not called.

Sample code that works with 0.3.7 and doesn't work with 1.0.0:

```python
from azure.keyvault import KeyVaultClient
from msrestazure.azure_active_directory import MSIAuthentication

issue_title: Applications stops with second Ctrl+C press if websocket session is active
issue_body:
I have a very simple example of websocket server. When I have no connected clients server stops on pressing Ctrl+C and prints output from on_cleanup callback, but when I have connected websocket clients first press of Ctrl+C does not stop app and second press of Ctrl+C stops app, but there is no output from on_cleanup callback.

Here is my code:

```
from aiohttp import WSCloseCode
import weakref
import aiohttp.web

HOST = '127.0.0.1'
PORT = 7777

class SimpleTerminal:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self._app = app
        self._ws = weakref.WeakSet()

    async def on_stop(self, app):
        print ('stop')
        for ws in self._ws:
            print (1)
            await ws.close(code=WSCloseCode.GOING_AWAY, message='Server shutdown')

    async def websocket_handler(self, request):
        print('Websocket connection starting')
        ws = aiohttp.web.WebSocketResponse()
        await ws.prepare(request)
        print('Websocket connection ready')

        self._ws.add(ws)
        try:
            async for msg in ws:
                print(msg)
                if msg.type == aiohttp.WSMsgType.TEXT:
                    print(msg.data)
                    if msg.data == 'close':
                        await ws.close()
                    else:
                        await ws.send_str(msg.data + '/answer')
        finally:
            print('Websocket connection closed')
            self._ws.discard(ws)

        return ws

def main():
    app = aiohttp.web.Application()
    simple_terminal = SimpleTerminal(app)
    app.router.add_route('GET', '/ws', simple_terminal.websocket_handler)
    app.on_cleanup.append(simple_terminal.on_stop)
    aiohttp.web.run_app(app, host=HOST, port=PORT)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

I use Fedora 27 with Python 3.6.5 and aiohttp 3.3.2

issue_title: Url gets manipulated in the wrong way

## Long story short

Url's passed into aiohttp gets changed in certain ways the servers don't always accept. In this case a `:` in the url which is quoted to `%3A` already, will get unquoted by aiohttp.

pass in a url with `%3A`. ## Your environment
aiohttp 2.2.5, python 3.5, linux

```python
import asyncio

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:

            await ws.receive_json()

async def ws_handler(req):

def main():

if __name__ == '__main__':

issue_title: Support Python 3.5
issue_body:
I don't see strong reasons to don't do it -- 3.5 has `await` syntax.

The motivation for 3.5 is: I want to drop 3.4 for most aio-libs projects in a few months.
Debian 9 Stretch has released with Python 3.5 stable support.
Thus people have native 3.5 on their linux boxes. It's crucial for projects like chat bots and https://home-assistant.io/ -- their users are very often not professional programmers and python 3.6 requirement with manual installation or using docker images is too hard for them.

On other hand I want to switch test suite for projects like aiopg to aiodocker, so test suite should be executed by every supported python version.

Is there strong objection against Python 3.5?

issue_title: ability to trust private pki
issue_body:
It would be handy if there was a method to add a root certificate to the bundle.

My particular case: Some 3rd party apps I am using rely on the requests package.  I cannot modify them directly to trust the internal pki.  I had to do the following:

```
pip install certifi

python
import certifi
certifi.where()
quit()

cat /path/to/trusted.pem >> /path/to/virtualenv/site-packages/certifi/cacert.pem
```

The problem with this is that updating the certifi package will overwrite the cacert.pem file and the app will break.

It would be much nicer if I could do something like the following at the top of my django settings.py file:
```
import certifi
certifi.add("/path/to/trusted.pem")
```

Then certifi would inspect the certificate to add.  If it was already included in "/path/to/virtualenv/site-packages/certifi/cacert.pem", nothing would happen.  Otherwise it would append the trusted certificate to the bundle.

issue_title: Lint to check post messages are translatable is throwing false positives
issue_body:
This lint is intended to detect not-translatable messages withing the method `message_post`, like this:
```
self.message_post(body='some message'))
```
And suggest using the function `_()` to make it translatable.

However, codes like the following are triggering false positives:
```
self.message_post(
    body=_('Error during the process: %s') % message,
    subtype='account.mt_invoice_validated')
```

Showing the following error message:
```
l10n_mx_edi_payslip/models/hr_payslip.py:264: [C8107(translation-required), HrPayslip.l10n_mx_edi_log_error] String parameter on "message_post" requires translation. Use body=_((_('Error during the process: %s')) % (message))
```

Not sure if this has anything to do with `message_post` and `_(...)` are located on different lines.

issue_title: grad is working, jacobian is not
issue_body:
First of all, autograd is an amazing tool! The grad function works great, but I am not able to make the `jacobian` function work.  I am on Python 3.4.3, numpy 1.10.1 and autograd 1.1.1 (pip).

The call to the `jacobian` function works, but the function it returns does not accept my argument and throws a `TypeError: The first input argument needs to be a sequence`

The error seems to occur in the concatenate function, defined at `line 44 in autograd/core.py` (although I do believe this error is actually an error raised by numpy). 

A minimum example to reproduce this:
```
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd import jacobian

x = np.array([5, 3], dtype=float)

def cost(x):
    return x[0]**2 / x[1] - np.log(x[1])

grad_cost = grad(cost)
jaco_cost = jacobian(cost)

print(grad_cost(x)) # works correctly!
print(jaco_cost(x)) # error
````
Another unrelated question (I am not sure if this is the correct place for this): If I wrote the above cost function in a separate module that imports numpy by itself (so not the autograd.numpy). Is it possible to somehow import this function from the module directly, instead of copy-pasting the code? Right now I get the following `AttributeError: 'FloatNode' object has no attribute 'log'`

The import is as follows:
```
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

#This module contains an 'import numpy as np' line
from my_module import cost

x = np.array([5, 3], dtype=float)
#Function from 'my_module' for which I'd like to calculate the gradient
grad_cost = grad(cost)
grad_cost(x) # error
```

If I remove the `np.log` call from my cost function the error disappears. So I guess I somehow need to override the 'regular' numpy import with the autograd numpy import, if possible.

Planemo could build a list of all the input parameter names, and check the ``<test>`` block's ``<param name="..."`` entries match.

Likewise, Planemo could build a list of all the output parameter names, and check the ``<test>`` block's ``<output name="..."`` entries match.

This would catch errors like https://github.com/peterjc/pico_galaxy/commit/81b7e0e2691a150d322b7eb05b447b343e4cbd72 (which failed at tool testing with a cryptic error).

In this example I had a typo (fasta vs fastq), the input parameter was:

``` xml
 <param name="input_fastq" type="data" format="fastq" label="FASTQ file to filter on the identifiers"/>
```

The test was using:

``` xml
<param name="input_fasta" value="sanger-pairs-mixed.fastq" ftype="fastq" />
```

It should have been:

``` xml
<param name="input_fastq" value="sanger-pairs-mixed.fastq" ftype="fastq" />
```

See also somewhat related #237, #258 and the meta-issue #13.

issue_title: reader: max_in_flight check in _send_rdy() doesn't take in-flight msgs into account
issue_body:
`Reader._send_rdy()` contains logic to avoid setting a `RDY` count on a connnection that would allow exceeding the reader's `max_in_flight`.  The [code](https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L455) in question is:
```
if (self.total_rdy + value) > self.max_in_flight
    if not conn.rdy:
        # if we're going from RDY 0 to non-0 and we couldn't because
        # of the configured max in flight, try again
        rdy_retry_callback = functools.partial(self._rdy_retry, conn, value)
        conn.rdy_timeout = self.io_loop.add_timeout(time.time() + 5, rdy_retry_callback)
    return
```
The condition `(self.total_rdy + value) > self.max_in_flight` doesn't take into account messages that are currently in flight.  This creates an opportunity for `max_in_flight` violations.  The recipe in [this report](https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/issues/175) will produce one, usually within a minute.

I reported this offline to @jonmorehouse, who pointed out that it was surprising the issue hasn't surfaced before.  I think the reason is that it can only hit when `max_in_flight` is less than the connection count, and this use case must be rare in practice:

* `_send_rdy()` is only called with a `value` parameter of 0, 1 or `_connection_max_in_flight()`.
* So if `len(conns) * _connection_max_in_flight() <= max_in_flight`, then we'll never violate the in-flight constraint, because we're implicitly restricting ourselves to a per-connection limit that guarantees safety.
* But if `len(conns) > max_in_flight` and ` _connection_max_in_flight() == 1`, then respecting the per-connection limit isn't enough.  In this case deciding whether a non-zero `RDY` count is permissible on a connection requires taking into account the in-flight count on other connections.

issue_title: Detect type hints: PEP484
issue_body:
*Original report by [w-thomas-q](https://launchpad.net/~w-thomas-q) on [Launchpad](https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/1587170):*

------------------------------------

Hi,

thanks for this really great tool!
One issues I stumbled upon was that type hints seem to be ignored / not detected.
For example, we have an import at the top of a file:

    from typing import List

And then later in an `__init__` function something like this:

    self.scripts = []  # type: List[script.Script]


Now running pyflakes (through flake8) gives me this error:

    foo.py:4:1: F401 'List' imported but unused


PEP484 defines this kind of type comments.
Is there an option to enable type hint detection?
Or can we have that as feature request? I guess I'm fine with type comments only for the time being, but of course a fully PEP484 compliant solution would be greatly appreciated :)

Thanks!

issue_title: Using pytest-cov for coverage results in top-level package name of "." when using coverage.py doesn't
issue_body:
First, versions:

```
$ python --version
Python 2.7.11
$ pip freeze|grep coverage
coverage==4.4.1
$ pip freeze|grep pytest-cov
pytest-cov==2.5.1
```

Happens on Ubuntu 12.04, Ubuntu 16.04, and Mac OS X El Capitan 10.11.6. Can't test anything else, but seems safe to say it would be the same.

I have a project laid out like this:

```
proj/ex_secure/__init__.py
proj/ex_secure/base.py
proj/ex_secure/metrics.py
proj/ex_secure/keys.py
proj/ex_secure/utils.py
proj/tests/test_base.py
proj/tests/test_metrics.py
proj/tests/test_keys.py
proj/.gitignore
proj/.pep8
proj/README.rst
proj/setup.cfg
proj/setup.py
```

All the example commands below I run from within the `proj` directory.

First, I run `coverage` directly:

```
$ coverage run --branch --source=ex_secure -m pytest -s --junitxml=pytests.xml
... output that doesn't matter ...
$ coverage xml
$ coverage report
Name                              Stmts   Miss Branch BrPart  Cover
-------------------------------------------------------------------
ex_secure/__init__.py                 1      0      0      0   100%
ex_secure/base.py                   274     55     74     12    74%
ex_secure/keys.py                    31      6      6      0    78%
ex_secure/metrics.py                 10      2      4      0    57%
ex_secure/util.py                   238    238     98      0     0%
```

And the top package of the XML file looks like so: 

```
<package branch-rate="0.3058" complexity="0" line-rate="0.4769" name="ex_secure">
```

Next, I run `pytest` with the `pytest-cov` plugin:

```
$ pytest -s --junitxml=pytests.xml --cov-report xml --cov-report term --cov-branch --cov=ex_secure
... output that doesn't matter ...
---------- coverage: platform darwin, python 2.7.11-final-0 ----------
Name                              Stmts   Miss Branch BrPart  Cover
-------------------------------------------------------------------
ex_secure/__init__.py                 1      0      0      0   100%
ex_secure/base.py                   274     55     74     12    74%
ex_secure/keys.py                    31      6      6      0    78%
ex_secure/metrics.py                 10      2      4      0    57%
ex_secure/util.py                   238    238     98      0     0%
```

And the top package of the XML file looks like so:

```
<package branch-rate="0.3058" complexity="0" line-rate="0.4769" name=".">
```

Notice the `name` of the package is now `.` instead of `ex_secure`. However, if I then run `coverage xml` to re-generate the report:

```
$ coverage xml
```

The XML file looks right again:

```
<package branch-rate="0.3058" complexity="0" line-rate="0.4769" name="ex_secure">
```

So I tried running `pytest` a different way:

```
$ pytest -s --junitxml=pytests.xml --cov-report xml --cov-report term --cov-branch --cov=ex_secure.base --cov=ex_secure.keys --cov=ex_secure.metrics --cov=ex_secure.util
... output that doesn't matter ...
---------- coverage: platform darwin, python 2.7.11-final-0 ----------
Name                              Stmts   Miss Branch BrPart  Cover
-------------------------------------------------------------------
ex_secure/base.py                   274     55     74     12    74%
ex_secure/keys.py                    31      6      6      0    78%
ex_secure/metrics.py                 10      2      4      0    57%
```

Now the XML also looks right:

```
<package branch-rate="0.4722" complexity="0" line-rate="0.6766" name="ex_secure">
```

But this has several downsides:
- I have to enumerate everything, and I miss coverage info for new files added if they are not enumerated.
- Can't cover `__init__.py`
- `utils.py` is not in the report anymore because none of its lines are covered, resulting in incorrectly high coverage results
- Branch rate and line rate are now different (because of missing `__init__.py` and `utils.py`

in `oscar/apps/checkout/session.py`, line 235:
`shipping_charge = D('0.00')`

In the next line shipping charge is passed to `get_order_totals`, which passes shipping charge to the `OrderTotalCalculator`.

`shipping_charge`cannot be a decimal object.

issue_title: Email to anonymous users cannot be saved
issue_body:
Basically `AbstractEmail.user` has `null=False` so there's no way to just simply save an email that's not hooked to a `User`.

Somewhat related, the actual email address that the email was sent to is not recorded. This could differ from a user's email because they could have changed it between when the email was generated and the present.

I'm not sure we should change the default behavior to save anonymous emails, but we should definitely allow for it to be overridden.

issue_title: Handle exceptions and interrupts for storage.py
issue_body: It is possible that an exception or signal could interrupt storage.py while it is modifying a lineage, leaving the associated files in an invalid state. Such critical sections of code should be identified and recovery methods should be written to attempt revert the lineage to a valid state before program exit.

issue_title: [LE.org/howitworks] Unavailable switch mentioned in documentation
issue_body:
In the [How It Works: Renewing a Certificate](https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/#renewing-a-certificate) on the LE homepage, the switch `--keep-until-renewal` is mentioned. However, this switch isn't available in the LE client, it's called `--keep-until-expiring`. I can't find the content of that page anywhere on GitHub; otherwise I would have made a PR.. :stuck_out_tongue:

issue_title: Handle ServerAlias when trying to match the best server name
issue_body:
Currently if you have an apache vhost file with a `VirtualHost` containing:

```
  ServerName site.com
  ServerAlias *.site.com
```

And pass `-d site.com,www.site.com` to the Let's Encrypt client, you get a "We were unable to find a vhost with a ServerName or Address of www.site.com" error message and even worse get a selection table.

This is because [_find_best_vhost()] (https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L341) is completely unaware of aliases.

issue_title: Presence packets clarification
issue_body:
Hi, this isn't really a bug, but more likely a misunderstanding on my part.

The API documentation reads:

> When user's me topic comes online (i.e. an authenticated session attaches to me topic), a {pres} packet is sent to me topics of all other users, who have peer to peer subscriptions with the first user.

My interpretation: User A is subscribed (and attached) to user B. User A is online and user B is offline.

User B logins in, and subscribes to their `me` topic.

User A receives a `{pres}` packet.

Currently, the only time I am seeing a `{pres}` packet show up, is when a user subscribes (and attaches) to another user for the first time.

Thanks for your time.

issue_title: Problem with sending emails with emojis
issue_body:
I got an issue with emails that contain emojis. When I receive email with emojis
all emojies are displayed like a bunch of '??'. I tried to send the same data via Postman
and email I received wasn't broken. So it seems someting wrong happens on the way 
between this python client and API server.
By digging into source code of `Transmissions` part and by sniffing traffic
I found that python's json module encodes (dumps) 'not simple' non-ascii characters 
(such emojies) to '\uXXXX' notation as a surrogate pair (as expected):

```
>>> emoji = u"😄"
>>> emoji
u'\U0001f604'
>>> print json.dumps(emoji)
"\ud83d\ude04"
```

By sending this data I got this emoji looks like:

```
\xed\xa0\xbd\xed\xb8\x84
```
And it's of course wrong. I sniffed traffic between Postman and API server and I found
that Postman sends json in utf-8 (without escaping to \uXXXX):

```
\xf0\x9f\x98\x84
```

So I repeat this for Python and it worked:
```
>>> json.dumps(emoji, ensure_ascii=False).encode('utf-8')
'"\xf0\x9f\x98\x84"'
```

I don't know on what side problem is but just my workaround to handle this problem.
And this Issue is just a point on a problem and its possible solution.

PS. There is no problem with sending emails consist of simple chars like '\u2605'.

issue_title: Options Object
issue_body:
I've got mixed thoughts on this, want to put it out there for discussion.

There are a bunch of GH issues regarding parsing that don't appear to have a clear "fix the parser" solution, require some type of user option.  A small amount of **kwarg proliferation is inevitable.  The suggestion here is to put these all into a `ParseOptions` class (or possibly private internal version thereof).  Three reasons:

1) Persistence.  Specify all your kwargs once and then pass the `opt = ParseOptions(...)` object on subsequent calls to `parse`.
1a) Possibly check for environment variables in case users want to do the "separation of code and config" thing.

2) Validation.  If there is kwarg proliferation, there are likely to be `if dayfirst and yearfirst: raise` type validations to be done.  This dove-tails into 1) if any of these are at all costly, doing them once instead of on each call to `parse` would be nice.

3) One object to pass around to internal methods.  Just a matter of convenience, if any of these kwargs become needed in `_parse` or `_could_be_tzname` or ... it'd be nice to have a single arg instead of a whole mess of them.

issue_title: Automatically calculate "id"
issue_body:
Currently, Graphs have to specify their ID. Ideally, the generator would just populate this uniquely.

Fixing this probably requires a shift in our internals. Rather than returning the "rendered" JSON dict we should return a custom object (e.g. a `Graph` type) with a `toJSON` method. When we have such objects, it will be fairly easy to add a post-processing stage to insert ID.

issue_title: Improve the consistence of the `utils.get_value()`
issue_body:
`utils.get_value()` is the entry-point for most of the fields accessor methods.

Its interface (aka. arguments order on a call) is different from a generic and common interface of a getter, be that for a `dict` or an `object`. This means that you can not overwrite this method with a simple assignment of `Field.get_attribute = dict.get` or `Field.get_attribute = getattr`. Instead you will need to create a new method that properly maps the arguments. This needs to be fixed... hopefully in 3.0.

This started here:
https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/7cba47481cbfb0f7e8b3fd36b303a419e236812a#commitcomment-14486377

Lets keep this task to remind us fixing it in the next major release.

issue_title: skip_on_fields_errors doesn't work for validates_schema
issue_body:
Here's a simple test case to reproduce the problem based on the example in the docs

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, validates_schema, ValidationError

class NumberSchema(Schema):
    field_a = fields.Integer()
    field_b = fields.Integer()

    @validates_schema
    def validate_numbers(self, data, skip_on_field_errors=True):
        if data['field_b'] >= data['field_a']:
            raise ValidationError('field_a must be greater than field_b')

schema = NumberSchema()
result, errors = schema.load({'field_a': 2, 'field_b': "bbbb"})
```
The `validate_numbers` method should be skipped because validation of `field_b` fails (since field_b contains a string). However, it is not skipped (which of course causes problems since that method expects `data['field_b']` to exist).

issue_title: Loading Datetime Objects
issue_body:
Maybe this is not so much of an issue but a misunderstanding on my side, so I'd think the following pytest-style unit test should not raise a validation error, since a `datetime.datetime` instance should satisfy the property of being a a datetime already:

```
from marshmallow import Schema, fields
import datetime


class SomeSchema(Schema):
    element = fields.DateTime()


def test_datetime_loading_accepts_datetime_object():
    schema = SomeSchema(strict=True)
    res = schema.load({"element": datetime.datetime.now()})
```

I do however get a validation error `marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError: {'element': ['Not a valid datetime.']}`

issue_title: Allow callables for fields.Nested
issue_body:
Suggested earlier here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/19#issuecomment-43685498.

The suggestion would be to allow:

```python
foo = fields.Nested(lambda: Foo)
```

in addition to

```python
foo = fields.Nested('Foo')
```

Seems a bit cleaner in general.

issue_title: Adding a parameter to allow 'conditional' attributes
issue_body:
In my models I have a `Event` that has a `leader` attribute, which is just a `User`. Not all Events have leaders though.
In the schema I have something like
```
class EventSchema(ma.Schema):
    location = ma.String()
    href = ma.URLFor('event_ep', event_id = '<id>')
    host = ma.Hyperlinks({
        'href': ma.URLFor('user_ep', user_id = '<host_id>')
    })
```
If there's no host (i.e., host_id is None) I'd like for EventSchema.host to be an empty dictionary, in effect skipping over the 'href' attribute.
Right now I've added a HostedEventSchema class that has the host attribute and inherits from EventSchema. When I serialize the event I check whether it has a host or not and then select the right schema.
1) Is there a more elegant way to do this in the current Marshmallow?
2) If not, does it make sense to add a parameter that would skip over an attribute if serializing either resulted in None or an Error?
I'll try looking into option 2) if nothing like that exists. Thanks.

Edit: I looked into the source code and I came upon the skip_missing parameter. However this still does not fix my problem because an AttributeError still results in an error with skip_missing = True. However I restructured my schema to use a nested HostSchema (with the href) and skip_missing = True. Still didn't work.

The bug seems to be that it would try to serialize a null object even if it didn't exist and skip_missing was true. For example, if Event.host didn't exist and skip_missing was True, it would try to serialize the nested object (None) without checking if host itself is none.

My proposed fix after line 581 in marshmallow/fields.py (https://github.com/sloria/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L581):

```
if getattr(obj, attr) is None:
     return {}
```

I didn't submit a pull request because I'd like feedback on this solution before writing tests.  Thoughts?

issue_title: schema.jsonify list of sqlalchemy instances incompatible with flask.jsonify
issue_body:
@ElvisTheKing @sloria 

continuing where #19 left off... 

I am trying to use flask-marshmallow schema.jsonify on the result of a sqlalchemy query

Using model instance is ok
``` 
from models.equipment import Equipment
equipments = Equipment.query.all()
equipments_schema.jsonify(equipments[0]).response

#('{\n  "date_added": "2015-10-23T11:07:46.384414+00:00", \n  "id": 1, \n  "name": "beacon"\n}', '\n')
```
List of model instances breaks
```
equipments_schema.jsonify(equipments)

#*** AttributeError: "id" is not a valid field for [<app.models.equipment.Equipment object at 0x7f5b3c11c240>, <app.models.equipment.Equipment object at 0x7f5b3c11cba8>].
```

versions

```
Flask==0.10.1
flask-marshmallow==0.6.2
-e git+https://github.com/flask-restful/flask-restful.git@54c51fd88759e2f398d566040200d2c9d51b8e5f#egg=Flask_RESTful-master
Flask-SQLAlchemy==2.0
marshmallow==2.1.3
marshmallow-sqlalchemy==0.6.0
```

I think it's because the sqlalchemy query returns a list of model instances, which is not compatible with flask.jsonify. There's some discussion in flask about this, but it sounds like that'll never be supported.

Am I missing something here? from the examples in the readme one might assume this should work. 

Otherwise, any thoughts for how to improve this?

issue_title: Precision@K values higher than 1
issue_body:
My understanding is that P@K values above 1 should not be possible, but I've managed to find a way. I'm fairly certain there are some constraints I'm missing in my problem setup, and looks like a good opportunity to update the docs and/or include some new error handling in the code (both of which I'm happy to help out with if I can).

Here's the basic setup, and I'll be happy to dig into details as much as necessary...
- I'm attempting to create a user matching recommender for a dating site. There are 6550 users and I'm treating users as items as well as users.
- I'm encoding right swipes as 1's and everything else as 0's (left or unswiped)
- I randomly assign 20% of all swipes to the test data. Not taking most recent, or ensuring that all users are represented in both train and test data
- I'm using the WARP loss function
- I have ~50 user features that I've extracted from their profile, but for testing purposes I've reduced the feature set down to: age, weight, and height. Weight and height have null values. None of these features have been normalized (is that necessary?)

When I fit the model without features (30 epochs, learning rate: .05) I get:
mean AUC: train 0.86, test 0.87   *** this is kinda odd too, test is always equal or higher
mean P@k: train 0.48, test 0.43

This seems to be pretty good, and when I examine the ranked recommendations they are appropriately all different for each user

If I add my user features (which I've tried both by adding as both user_features and item_features, and also just as user_features), I get:
mean AUC: train 0.88, test 0.89
mean P@k: train 11.41, test 2.96

Seems impossible. And also - every user's top 5 recommendations are out of the same set of 6-7 "items". There's no variation.

Does anything stick out as being obviously wrong conceptually?

issue_title: id field set on ModelSerializer is bypassed and not used
issue_body:
We noticed that the `id = serializers.SerializerMethodField()` that we have set on our `ModelSerializer` is not being used when this package creates the json-api structure. Upon investigating I saw this line which appears to use the `resource_object.pk` for the `id` field:

https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/develop/rest_framework_json_api/renderers.py#L387

```
class FooSerializer(ModelSerializer):
    id = serializers.SerializerMethodField()

    def get_id(self, obj):
        """
        Returns the Foo.short_id as `id` for the client.
        """
        return shortuuid.serialize_uuid(obj.id).decode('utf-8')
```

This causes a bug where if someone has written a custom serialized `id` field it is bypassed completely. In our case we are customizing the id of our model for the client.

We may create a shortcut locally but I think this package should respect anything defined in the local serializers and use that rather than bypassing it. It also brings up a question on using this package in general: does this package change the way fields must be defined compared to the typical DRF way? Does it automatically translate the normal DRF serializer fields into the appropriate json-api versions? I realize it's somewhat complex so just trying to gauge how this will affect writing serializers for our project

`awsume -r` should reload credentials that were previous acquired with `awsume -a` flag
issue_body:
`awsume -r <role>` reloads <role> credentials, but `awsume -r` expects a profile name. 

I think `awsume -r` without a role name should reload _ALL_ profiles that were previously executed with `awsume -a <role_name>`. This way I don't have to remember which profiles I had previously configured to automatically refresh.

Moreover, I think `awsume -r <rolename>` should act l like `awsume -a <rolename>; awsume -r <rolename>` if `awsume -a` was **not** run previously.

Great program! I love it and use it daily for my consulting work. Keep up the good work.

issue_title: Tee should act as proxy to the wrapped output
issue_body:
In my case I was downloading a file using `wget.download(url, file_name)`. It touches `sys.stdout.fileno()` to get the window size for the progress bar. However sacred wraps stdout using `Tee` which doesn't have the `fileno()` method.

A workaround was to monkey patch it from outside.

```
from sacred.utils import Tee
Tee.fileno = lambda self: self.out1.fileno()
```

Better would be to proxy these call either manually or automatically to the underlying main output stream.

```
class Tee:
  # ...
  def fileno(self.):
    return self.out1.fileno()
```

issue_title: ID3 version header not updatable
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, there is no way to update the ID3 version header (bytes 4 and 5 of the file) using mutagen.  
Steps for replication:  
* Create an MP3 file with ID3v2.3 tags and headers
* Load it into mutagen with `f = mutagen.id3.ID3("with-v23.mp3")`. The documentation states this automatically runs the `update_to_v24()` function, which converts all tags to their v2.4 equivalents.
* Save the file with `f.save()`  

The first five bytes of the file *should* now be `49 44 33 04 00`, but are actually `49 44 33 03 00`.  
Additionally, I tried to manually set the version with:  
* `f.version = (2, 4, 0)`
* `f._version = (2, 4, 0)`  

Both of those operations resulted in no change to `f.version`'s value, and running `f.save()` after each also did not update the header on disk. Based on the presence of a setter for `ID3.version` [here](https://github.com/quodlibet/mutagen/blob/master/mutagen/id3/_file.py#L87), I don't understand why setting it doesn't do anything.  
Although it's a semi-unusual corner case, this bug prevents files created with ID3v2.3 tags from being updated to use ID3v2.4 features (like chapters), even when all tags present in the file are from ID3v2.4.

issue_title: Picard slowness over Windows network share
issue_body:
Using Picard over a Windows 10 network share I have been getting very poor performance. Windows task manager shows open handles growing.

This issue is to log the possibility of a file handle leak in Mutagen creating these performance problems.

Ongoing discussion re: Picard is in ticket [PICARD-744](https://tickets.metabrainz.org/browse/PICARD-744) but for MP3 files it looks to me like the code reads the header to determine the ID3 data size, and at ID3/_file.py:164 calls ._util.py:read_full which reads the rest of the ID3 data (using the size in the header) but does NOT read to the end of the file (which would implicitly close the file) or does it close the file explicitly.

I haven't looked at other formats, but this may apply to them as well as ID3.

Do we need to explicitly close the file at the end of the `load` method?

issue_title: Will Z3 ever "collapse" model entries in v2 format?
issue_body:
Consider the following simple input file:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(declare-fun myfun ( Int ) Int)
(assert (! (= (myfun 1) 1)))
(assert (! (= (myfun 2) 2)))
(assert (! (= (myfun 3) 1)))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
When I run this through Z3 using the "model.v2=true" option, I get the following output:
```
sat
"myfun -> {
  1 -> 1
  2 -> 2
  3 -> 1
  else -> 1
}"
```
In this case, Z3 did not "collapse" the two entries that yield the value 1; every application of the function to a unique set of arguments is explicitly listed.  Is this guaranteed to always be the case, or can there be cases where collapsing can occur?

Thanks!

issue_title: Proofs without Abbreviation
issue_body: Z3's proofs contain many subformula abbreviations, through let statements that introduce new names of the form "$x..." . While this has the advantage of making the proof more compact, it also has the disadvantage of making the proof harder for humans to read. Is there an option to disable these abbreviations in the proof production? If not, could you implement it?

issue_title: z3py remove specific assertions

issue_title: Is there interest for flexible training loops, batch iteration schemes, etc
issue_body:
Would the Lasagne community / developers be interested in adding code for training loops, basic DNN construction, etc?

I found that code for training loops with features such as early termination, etc would involve a lot of copy+paste, so I put together a library that can be found at:

http://github.com/Britefury/britefury-lasagne-helpers

I find it to be very helpful when I need to throw a network together and train/use it.

I would like to know if the Lasagne developers would be interested in taking some of this code and incorporating it into Lasagne?

My code has no documentation beyond the doc-strings; this would need to be worked on.

What's there:

## `data_souce` module
Defines the `batch_iterator` function that extracts mini-batches from a data set which can be NumPy arrays or other sources of data; the protocol is defined in the doc-strings.
Code for iterating though a dataset looks like:
```
for (batch_X, batch_y) in data_source.batch_iterator([trainX, train_y], batchsize=128)
    ...
```
where `train_X` and `train_y` can be NumPy arrays or other objects that support `__len__` and `__getitem__`; for more info see the protocols defined in `data_source`

## `trainer` module
Defines the `Trainer` class that implements a reasonably flexible training loop. It monitors the 
validation score of the network and can terminate early if the score doesn't improve within a certain number of epochs. It also reports training progress and saves the state of the network when validation score improves. Its train method accepts data sources and uses the `data_source` module to get batches. Setting up the trainer looks like:
```
train_batch = theano.function(...) # should update params and return train loss
eval_batch = theano.function(...) # should evaluate the performance and return loss/err rate

trainer = trainer.Trainer()

# Provide training function
trainer.train_with(train_batch_fn=train_batch)

# Provide evaluation function
trainer.evaluate_with(eval_batch_fn=eval_batch)

# Set the verbosity; can be VERBOSITY_NONE (nothing), VERBOSITY_MINIMAL (final performance only),
VERBOSITY_EPOCH (report every epoch), VERBOSITY_BATCH (report each batch for those large datasets)
trainer.report(verbosity=trainer.VERBOSITY_EPOCH)

# Have the trainer save the state of the network after each improvement in validation score
trainer.retain_best_scoring_state_of_network(final_layer)

# Train for a maximum of 100 epochs, minimum of 50, if training proceeds for 35 epochs without any improvement in validation score, stop early
trainer.train_for(num_epochs=100, min_epochs=50, val_improve_num_epochs=35)

# Train it
trainer.train(train_set=[train_X, train_y], val_set=[val_X, val_y], test_set=[test_X, test_y], batchsize=128)
```

## `basic_dnn` module
Defines the `BasicDNN` class that defines a neural network that can have multiple inputs and multiple targets. Uses objectives defined in the `dnn_objective` module to provide targets to optimise and generate the loss function. Also defines the functions `classifier`, `simple_classifier`, `regressor` and `simple_regressor`. So setting up an image classifier is as easy as:

```
# Define a function that build the network according to the architecture that we need
def build_network(input_vars):
    in_var = input_vars[0] if input_vars is not None else None
    input_layer = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(shape=(None, 3, 48, 48), input_var=in_var)
    ...
    return final_layer

# doing this will build the network, set up loss functions, optimisation, etc
clf = basic_dnn.simple_classifier(
    build_network,           # network builder function
    n_input_spatial_dims=2,           #images are two-dimensional
    n_target_spatial_dims=0,           #simple classifier, just one prediction
    target_channel_index=None,           #if we give an index, then the target variable needs to be of shape `(sample,channel)` where this index is passed as `channel`
    score='err',           # score measure; can also be 'jaccard', 'f1', etc
    updates_fn=lambda cost, params: lasagne.updates.adam(cost, params, learning_rate=lr)          # updates function
)

clf.trainer.train_for(num_epochs=100, min_epochs=20, val_improve_num_epochs=35)

# train_X: (sample,channel,height,width)
# train_y: (sample,)
# similar for val_* and test_*
clf.trainer.train([train_X, train_y], [val_X, val_y], [test_X, test_y], batchsize=128)
```

`simple_classifier` is quite flexible; it can also be used to produce two-dimensional (e.g. pixel-wise) predictions in order to build something like an FCN segmentation network; change the `n_target_spatial_dims` parameter to 2:

```
fcn_clf = basic_dnn.simple_classifier(
    build_network,
    n_input_spatial_dims=2,
    n_target_spatial_dims=2,           #predict per pixel
    target_channel_index=None,`(sample,channel)` where this index is passed as `channel`
    score='err',
    updates_fn=lambda cost, params: lasagne.updates.adam(cost, params, learning_rate=lr)
)

clf.trainer.train_for(num_epochs=100, min_epochs=20, val_improve_num_epochs=35)

# train_X: (sample,channel,height,width)
# train_y: (sample,height,width)
# similar for val_* and test_*
clf.trainer.train([train_X, train_y], [val_X, val_y], [test_X, test_y], batchsize=128)
```

The BasicDNN class allows for some more flexibility, so that multiple training objectives can be set up; e.g. a combination of classification and regression targets for e.g. Fast RCNN localiser. The objectives are defined in the `dnn_objectives` module.

## `image_window_extractor` and `tiling_scheme` modules
This code is usable but slightly less developed so far. Provides classes for extracting patches from a set of images in a reasonably memory efficient manner. `ImageWindowExtractor` object support the `__len__` and `__getitem__` method so they can be passed as part of datasets, e.g. they can be passed to `Trainer.train` as `train_X`, or `train_y`; the batch iteration system will draw data from them as is.

## `work_pool` module
This introduces a dependency on `joblib` and is probably beyond the scope of Lasagne. It provides functionality for spreading tasks among multiple processes and uses joblib's worker pool that uses shared memory to move data between processes quickly. Its intended use case is generating mini-batches of data in separate processes where this could be an expensive operation due to having to load data from disc, perform data augmentation, etc.


I don't know how much of this is of interest to you guys, but I would be happy to collaborate in order to incorporate it into Lasagne if you're up for it.


## `pretrained` package
Provides code for building VGG and ResNet-50 (so far) networks, downloading weights, etc. The code is adapted from `Lasagne-recipes`. Main use cases is grabbing an existing network and using in a transfer learning scenario.

issue_title: MoneyField breaks Admin List view for prices with certain currencies
issue_body:
I included a standard money field in the Admin list view.
models.py:

```
    price = MoneyField('Unit price', max_digits=8, decimal_places=2, 
                       default_currency='USD',
                       blank=True, null=True, 
                       help_text = 'cost per unit (!)')
```

admin.py:
`list_display = ('product',  'Status', 'show_quantity',  'price' )
`
This works for the default USD prices but it breaks as soon as I add, e.g. a Saudi Riyal price. The problem seems to be that the admin view interprets MoneyField as a DecimalField and then tries to format o.price as a decimal number whereas the string actually already contains the arabic currency symbol.:

> File "/data/raik/py/labhamsterdjango/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_list.py", line 231, in items_for_result
>   result_repr = display_for_field(value, f, empty_value_display)
> File "/data/raik/py/labhamsterdjango/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/contrib/admin/utils.py", line 399, in display_for_field
>   return formats.number_format(value, field.decimal_places)
> File "/data/raik/py/labhamsterdjango/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/utils/formats.py", line 181, in number_format
>   force_grouping=force_grouping
> File "/data/raik/py/labhamsterdjango/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/utils/numberformat.py", line 38, in format
>   int_part, dec_part = str_number.split('.')

Workaround:
defining a custom show_price method prevents this:

admin.py:

```
    list_display = ('product',  'Status', 'show_quantity', 'show_price',)
    def show_price(self, o):
        """Workaround for bug in djmoney -- MoneyField confuses Admin formatting"""
        if not o.price:
            return u''
        return unicode(o.price)
```
