issue_title: Angr finds path but flag is wrong
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm new to Angr and I was playing with it for the first time with the CSAW 2016 rock crackme. Its a very simple crackme where we have to hit an address avoiding another one within a loop. In order to do that, I have basically make the stdin symbolic, added some constraints about printable characters and use the path_group explorer to find a path that hits the Win address avoiding the Fail one:

```
import angr, simuvex

def main():
    p = angr.Project("rock", load_options={'auto_load_libs': True})
    #s = p.factory.entry_state(args=['./rock'], add_options=simuvex.o.unicorn, remove_options={simuvex.o.LAZY_SOLVES})
    # s = p.factory.blank_state(addr=0x401260, remove_options={simuvex.o.LAZY_SOLVES})
    s = p.factory.full_init_state(args=['./rock'], remove_options={simuvex.o.LAZY_SOLVES})

    # input needs to be 30 bytes long and new line
    for i in xrange(30):
        k = s.posix.files[0].read_from(1)
        s.se.add(k>0x20)
        s.se.add(k<0x7E)
        s.se.add(k!=0xA)
    # last character is new line
    k = s.posix.files[0].read_from(1)
    s.se.add(k==0xA)

    # reset the symbolic stdin's properties and set its length
    s.posix.files[0].seek(0)
    s.posix.files[0].length = 31

    pg = p.factory.path_group(s)
    pg.explore(find=0x40149e, avoid=0x40186d)

    print pg

    if len(pg.errored) > 0:
        print list(pg.errored[0].trace)
        print pg.errored[0].state
        pg.errored[0].retry()

    if len(pg.found) > 0:
        found_state = pg.found[0].state
        #import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()
        return found_state.posix.dumps(0)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print main()
```

I have tried several initializations, but finally using the full init one since its a c++ binary and Im not sure about Angr support in terms of SimProcedures, etc.

When I run it, It finds a path but the flag is wrong:

```
(angr_pypy) ~/CTFs/tasks/CSAW2k16/rock❯ python solve_rock_angr.py
<PathGroup with 82 active, 6 deadended, 1 found>
$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$
```

The binary can be found [here](https://github.com/pwntester/csaw2016_rock) and a write up of the challenge [here](https://github.com/ernw/ctf-writeups/tree/master/csaw2016/rock).

Please do not waste any time on this if you don't spot the error immediately, Im sure you all have better things to do :D

Thanks,
A

issue_title: crash in cfg_base._analyze_function_features
issue_body:
The line https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/c60388cddc93ec2c11a28bd0ff95b4cd5ba979f2/angr/analyses/cfg_base.py#L607 crashes angr if `target_func` is None. This happens if the `target_addr` argument of `self.kb.functions.function(addr=target_addr)` is not found. 
The root cause seems to be that `target_addr = edges[0][1].addr` not necessarily returns the first address of a function.
I can fix this issue by searching for the member of edges which is of class angr.knowledge.function.Function. The question remains is whether there are other assumptions violated, e.g. is the iteration in:

`edges = [ edge for edge in func.transition_graph.edges(
                                                nbunch=[src], data=True
                                                ) if edge[2]['type'] != 'fake_return'
]`

supposed to return the entrypoint of the function in the first element?

issue_title: CFG-Support for MachO
plt stubs not processed properly in CFG on ELF ARMv7
issue_body:
In the make_functions method of CFGBase, the following code processes plt stubs and expects them to be aligned to 16-byte boundaries:

```
        # Remove all stubs after PLT entries
        to_remove = set()
        for fn in self.kb.functions.values():
            addr = fn.addr - (fn.addr % 16)
```

On ARMLEv7 ELF files the PLT stubs aren't necessarily aligned on 16-byte boundaries, and as a result many PLT functions (in my sample, all PLT functions with addresses ending in 0xC) are missing in the eventual functions dictionary produced by the CFG analysis. Changing this alignment to 4 appears to fix the issue.

issue_title: Feature Request:  Access to Mavlink messages received by Autopilot
issue_body:
For Companion Computers (e.g. Raspberry Pi) running DroneKit onboard an aircraft, it's easy to access outgoing mavlink messages that the autopilot sends to the GCS.  You can do this by simply invoking DroneKit's set_mavlink_callback() function.  However, there doesn't appear to be any way in DroneKit to intercept mavlink messages going in the other direction -- messages that the GCS sends to the Autopilot (e.g. MAV_CMD_DO_SET_MODE). 

This is particularly limiting for mavlink messages that the autopilot doesn't ACK, as there doesn't seem to be any way for the Companion Computer to know if a specific message was sent from the GCS to the Autopilot.

issue_title: Make a note of the correct units for observer altitude in docs
issue_body:
Hello,

After about an hour of frustration, I discovered that get_observer_look expects the observer location to be in feet, not in meters. The PyOrbital azimuth calculation was matching the prediction from GPredict and my own MATLAB code, but the elevation was consistently off by a factor of about 3 (and 1m = 3.28ft). 

The documentation on [readthedocs](http://pyorbital.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) makes no mention of units for the altitude value. Please update it to include them so we can prevent the same frustration for others in the future.
Thanks!

Relevant conversation from Slack:

issue_title: click_log only applies to the initial module's logger.
issue_body:
On an app with more than one logger, click_log will configure the logger for the initial module (where `click_log.basic_config` is called), but not other modules.

This results in `click_log` effectively having no effect on other modules.

A potential solution for this, would be to make `basic_config` take `logger` as an OPTIONAL parameter.
If `logger == None`, then configure `logging` (which basically means, override global defaults).

issue_title: Several content type renderers? (rss, geojson, ...)
issue_body:
Using Cornice it is possible to define several renderers per view. 

```python
@records.get(permission='get_records')
@records.get(accept='application/vnd.geo+json', renderer='geojson', permission='get_records')
def get_records(request):
    ....
    return [...]
```

The renderer will be chosen based on `Accept` request header.

Using Cornice/Cliquet resources, it does not seem obvious to define several views, since it is done during [resource registration](https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/3.1.1/cliquet/resource/__init__.py#L97-L98)

What would be the best way to have a plugin that renders a specific output (e.g. rss/geojson) on the GET records endpoint ?

/cc @almet

issue_title: Rows from the timestamps table are never deleted
issue_body:
When we delete a bucket, all its descendants are deleted. But the `timestamps` table will still retain the row for all its collections and groups.

For clients, it means that when you recreate a collection, it will reuse the previous timestamp instead of a new one.

issue_title: Create Collection event notification not fired for auto-create on default bucket
issue_body:
I was hacking around with Redis event notifications today. I setup two separate listeners with filters:

```
kinto.event_listeners = redis1 redis2

kinto.event_listeners.redis1.use = cliquet.listeners.redis
kinto.event_listeners.redis1.url = <url>
kinto.event_listeners.redis1.pool_size = 5
kinto.event_listeners.redis1.listname = kinto:collection:create
kinto.event_listeners.redis1.actions = create
kinto.event_listeners.redis1.resources = collection

kinto.event_listeners.redis2.url = <url>
kinto.event_listeners.redis2.pool_size = 5
kinto.event_listeners.redis2.listname = kinto:record:create
kinto.event_listeners.redis2.actions = create
kinto.event_listeners.redis2.resources = record
```

To test, I was using the default bucket, and the inserting a sample record to a collection that did not yet exist. Although the collection was auto-created, and the record inserted, only the event for create record was logged to Redis. If I create the collection manually, then the create collection event is logged.

Summary: Auto-created collections on the default bucket are not firing event notifications.

issue_title: Should we remove kinto.core features unused in Kinto ?
issue_body:
`kinto.core` is the toolkit leveraged by Kinto but that can be used to implement domain-specific APIs. It ships a lot of good practices by default.

Historically, `kinto.core` was *Cliquet* a toolkit in a separate repo.
It has features that were implemented for the *readinglist*, *Syncto* and *kinto* projects.

But now it is the core of Kinto, should we keep core features that are unused ?

For example:
* [readonly fields in resources](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/core/resource/schema.py#L24-L29)
* [resources without permissions (records per user-id)](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/core/resource/__init__.py#L126)
* [private permission in authz policy](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/core/authorization.py#L14-L15)
* [newrelic monitoring agent](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/core/initialization.py#L289-L294)

In other words, should we get rid of these now, and re-introduce them the day we need them, or should we keep them ­— just in case ?

issue_title: Build a Database with Known Entities
issue_body:
It could be cool to have scripts that can build a Database with known entities from Wikidata.

E.g. one could use a SPARQL query like this

```
SELECT ?subj ?label (GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT(?altLabel); separator=", ") as ?altlabels)
WHERE
{
	?subj wdt:P31 wd:Q215380 .
	SERVICE wikibase:label {
		bd:serviceParam wikibase:language "en" .
		?subj rdfs:label ?label .
		?subj skos:altLabel ?altLabel            
	}
}
GROUP BY ?subj ?label
LIMIT 100
```

to select all bands in Wikidata. 
Then those entities could be stored in a Trie (as done currently) and the trie nodes could hold the query entity (e.g. Q215380) as well as the subject identifier (for example, Dire Straits are wd:Q50040)

For the intent matching they could be used as additional information for the probabilities (e.g. `optional(Adapt.MusicEntity)`)

issue_title: Support temporary language switch inside a request
issue_body:
It would be nice to be able to stash the current language somewhere and use another one for a while during a request execution.

The main use-case I see is sending out emails with Flask-Email. The email should be translated according to the recipient's language and not the current user's language.

Something like:

    from flaskext.babel import stash, unstash

    # do stuff with current user's language
    print _('foo')
    # Now switch to th recipient language
    stash(new_lang=recipient.language)
    send email with i18n tags
    unstash()
    # Now the language is back to the current user's lang

Well the API is terrible, but you get the idea..

issue_title: Circular import issue with NonNull or required=True
issue_body:
Our project consists of a number of modules, some of which refer to eachother in a circular manner. We have until now been able to resolve any circular import issues by using strings to refer to schema objects where necessary. However, when attempting to make some fields required, we are encountering errors that appear to be caused by circular imports.

I have tried the solutions presented in the following issues, but to no avail.
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/522
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/110
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/436

Here is a simple example which illustrates the problem:

```python
# test/things.py
class Book(graphene_django.DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = BookModel

class Library(graphene_django.DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = LibraryModel
        exclude_fields = ('bookmodel_set', 'headlibrarianmodel')

    books = graphene.List(graphene.NonNull('test.things.Book'), required=True)
    head_librarian = graphene.NonNull(HeadLibrarian)

# test/people.py
class HeadLibrarian(graphene_django.DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = HeadLibrarianModel
        exclude_fields = ('the_library',)

    # This works ...
    # library = graphene.Field('test.things.Library')

    # ... but the following don't.

    # ImportError: Module "test.things" does not define a "Library" attribute/class
    library = graphene.Field('test.things.Library', required=True)

    # ImportError: Module "test.things" does not define a "Library" attribute/class
    # library = graphene.NonNull('test.things.Library')

    # ImportError: Module "test.things" does not define a "Library" attribute/class
    # library = graphene.Field(graphene.NonNull('test.things.Library'))

    # AttributeError: 'Field' object has no attribute '_meta'
    # library = graphene.NonNull(graphene.Field('test.things.Library'))

```

I have also attached a Dockerfile which contains the complete running example. Build and run the image as follows to reproduce the problem:
```
docker build -t graphene-bug-test .
docker run -it --name gbt graphene-bug-test`
docker exec gbt ./manage.py dumpschema`
```

We are using `django==1.11` and `graphene_django==1.3` but since this seems to be an issue in core graphene, not specific to graphene_django, I have reported it here.



[Dockerfile.txt](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/files/1361442/Dockerfile.txt)

issue_title: Attempts to change config values fail silently and appear to succeed when user does not have change permissions
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

Users with `is_superuser` set to `False` can view constance configs, but not edit them, by default. The constance admin interface UI shows no indication that the fields are readonly, or that the user does not have permission to edit them, and even makes it seem like the user has edited the field when they try to save changes.

This took a while to debug because it fails completely silently with no error message. As such I feel it could be friendlier.

Additionally, I'm not sure that letting non-superuser users view constance config values is a sensible default.

The issue in my case was solved by giving the staff user permissions, but this was found only on second reading of the documentation, as there's only a few lines that indicate anything about permissions in the docs, and no messaging in the admin interface indicates why attempts to change the constance configs failed.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install constance
2. Access the admin interface as a user who a) is not a superuser, and b) does not have constance edit permissions.
3. View the constance config in the admin interface.
4. Attempt to edit the constance config (it will appear that the constance config has changed).
5. Go to a different area of the admin site and return to the constance configs.
6. At this point it will be apparent that the config has not changed.

### System configuration

* Django version: 1.8.18
* Python version: 2.7.14
* Django constance version: 2.1.0

issue_title: Race condition in creation of Access Token with external OAuth2 server
issue_body:
In [oauth2_validators._get_token_from_authentication_server](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/master/oauth2_provider/oauth2_validators.py#L335-L350) there's a race condition when two requests both present a new Bearer Access Token causing a SQL insertion error for a uniqueness constraint violation. 

This has been exercised in the wild by our project that has a client that makes near simultaneous requests of the same Django endpoint with the same Bearer token (see [validate_bearer_token](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/master/oauth2_provider/oauth2_validators.py#L384-L402). 

I believe the fix is to use `AccessToken.objects.get_or_create()`. Does this make sense @jleclanche?

issue_title: cf sync hanging for s3 bucket policy
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm running cfn-sphere 0.1.35 on Mac OS X 10.11.6. I have the following single stack template that creates an S3 bucket and a policy for that bucket:

```
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: S3 bucket and policy to test hanging behaviour
Resources:
  hangS3Bucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: '|ref|AWS::StackName'
  hangS3BucketPolicy:
    Type: AWS::S3::BucketPolicy
    Properties:
      Bucket: '|ref|hangS3Bucket'
      PolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          -
            Action:
              - 's3:GetObject'
            Effect: 'Allow'
            Resource:
              Fn::Join:
                - ""
                -
                  - "arn:aws:s3:::"
                  - '|ref|hangS3Bucket'
                  - "/*"
            Principal: '*'
```

When I run this with cf sync, the s3 bucket is created in about 30 seconds, but then the bucket policy hangs for 9 minutes 30 seconds until timing out.

If I modify the template slightly to be:

```
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: S3 bucket and policy to test hanging behaviour
Resources:
  hangS3Bucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: '|ref|AWS::StackName'
  hangS3BucketPolicy:
    Type: AWS::S3::BucketPolicy
    Properties:
      Bucket: '|ref|hangS3Bucket'
      PolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          -
            Action:
              - 's3:GetObject'
            Effect: 'Allow'
            Resource:
              Fn::Join:
                - ""
                -
                  - "arn:aws:s3:::"
                  -
                    Ref: "hangS3Bucket"
                  - "/*"
            Principal: '*'
```

then there is no hanging at all, with the S3 bucket and the bucket policy being created in 37 seconds.

The only difference between these two templates is that the first uses '|ref|hangS3Bucket' within an Fn::Join statement inside the bucket policy document resource and the second uses Ref: "hangS3Bucket" instead.

Does using the '|ref|resource_id' style of referencing cause hanging behaviour within an Fn::Join statement? Running the first stack template does not give any errors about invalid template syntax, but merely runs for 10 minutes with no output after "hangS3BucketPolicy: CREATE_IN_PROGRESS:" and then times out.

issue_title: spinel: documentation issue with local routes property
issue_body:
PROP_THREAD_LOCAL_ROUTES (route add/remove) looks to have the same arguments as PROP_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS (prefix add/remove), but the stable parameter should probably be               prf instead: Default Router Preference, which may be: 'high', 'med', or 'low'.

Also, for PROP_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS, the stable flag should probably just be part of the general flags parameter and follow the Thread specification definition, otherwise the API has a  redundant and possibly conflicting way of accessing it.

```
D.4.12. PROP 91: PROP_THREAD_LOCAL_ROUTES

Type: Read-Write
Packed-Encoding: A(T(6CbC))
Data per item is:

6: IPv6 Prefix
C: Prefix length, in bits
b: Stable flag
C: Other flags
```

issue_body: Old version of to_categorical took 2 parameters, the second one is nb_classes, why should it be removed from new version? What are the benefits of doing this? In my opinion, sometimes we must list all the labels in a list_file, but that do not mean we have all labels in each dataset, e.g. we may not see any sample in testset for small categories.  Really do not understand.

issue_title: `video()` does not work with Python 3 and OpenCV 3
issue_body:
The newly implemented `video()` is not compatible with Python 3 and OpenCV 3. 

In OpenCV 3, `fourcc` is a new API `cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc()`. Could you maybe add an if-else condition for this ?

issue_title: Legend is missing
issue_body: In version 0.1.8.3 the plots are not including legends, even when passing them explicitly with the legend property. It was working before, do you know what might be going on?

issue_title: How to explain the text classification model?
issue_body: Explain the text classification model is hard, since fastText doesn't provides a tool to export the model. It's just like a black box.

issue_title: scipy interface patching.
issue_body: You may find https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/5295 helpful (TLDR: patching scipy's fftpack is essentially a simple `scipy.fftpack = pyfftw.interfaces.scipy_fftpack` now) and update the docs accordingly.

issue_title: Feature: if no sheet is declared with in2csv then process all sheets in .xls or xlsx files
issue_body: Yeah. If no sheetname is specified, loop through all sheets in the Excel file and output a csv for each file.

issue_title: Requiring metaclass attribute breaks a lot of code
issue_body:
I'm sorry to not have caught this earlier, but I'm really not happy with the change to require `py::metaclass()` whenever a static property is used.
For very large codebases like ours, where many many classes have static properties this causes unnecessary breakage. Is there any way this can be avoided now or in the future?

issue_title: Provide overload similar to return_value_policy to unlock the GIL
Problem with 0-dim numpy arrays
issue_body:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/534b756cb3244aca959359c07d44e4fed3498ba8/include/pybind11/numpy.h#L771

It seems to me 0-dim numpy array are supported by numpy quite well. This is not the case with the pybind11 binding however. At least this line fails due to the use of an unsigned type that underflows for 0-dim arrays.

issue_title: test_std_exception sometimes fails when built with clang-cl on Windows
issue_body:
Make sure you've completed the following steps before submitting your issue -- thank you!

1. Check if your question has already been answered in the [FAQ](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html) section.
2. Make sure you've read the [documentation](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/). Your issue may be addressed there.
3. If those resources didn't help and you only have a short question (not a bug report), consider asking in the [Gitter chat room](https://gitter.im/pybind/Lobby).
4. If you have a genuine bug report or a more complex question which is not answered in the previous items (or not suitable for chat), please fill in the details below.
5. Include a self-contained and minimal piece of code that reproduces the problem. If that's not possible, try to make the description as clear as possible.

*After reading, remove this checklist and the template text in parentheses below.*

## Issue description

(Provide a short description, state the expected behavior and what actually happens.)
Building pybind11 with Clang on Windows sometimes crashes on the test `test_std_exception` and some similar tests that throw a `RuntimeError`. Not sure if it's a bug here or at the clang side.

## Reproducible example code

(The code should be minimal, have no external dependencies, isolate the function(s) that cause breakage. Submit matched and complete C++ and Python snippets that can be easily compiled and run to diagnose the issue.)
1. Download LLVM toolchain 6.0.0 x64 for Windows from this [site](http://releases.llvm.org/) and install
2. Download MSVC 2017 BuildTools for [here](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/zh-hans/thank-you-downloading-visual-studio/?sku=BuildTools&rel=15) and install
3. Download Python 3.5/3.6 and install
4. Make sure the LLVM and Python directory is added to PATH
5. Activate the x64 VS dev command prompt "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\2017\BuildTools\VC\Auxiliary\Build\vcvars64.bat"
6. Install ninja-build `pip install ninja`
7. Set environmental variables for build 
```CMD
set CC=clang-cl
set CXX=clang-cl
```
8. Some sources in the test suite are not able to compile, including `test_stl_binders.cpp` and `test_numpy_dtypes.cpp`. Let's quote them out.
9. Let's start the building process. 
```cmd
mkdir build
cd build
cmake .. -GNinja
```
10. Run the test file `test_exceptions.py` multiple times and sometimes it will crash.

I get this 404 NOT FOUND error while syncing.

My calendar is on Fruux(.com), but I used Thunderbird before where I also had a Google Calendar. I think somehow a reference to a Google Calendar item must have slipped into my Fruux data and vdirsyncer cannot handle that.

issue_title: tokenize, JSON, _display
issue_body:
- tokenize.generate_tokens seems to only be used in python2 according to docs.python.org and new version is supposed to be tokenize.tokenize? However changing it to new version seems to create an error "startswith first arg must be str or a tuple of str, not bytes". Looking at past pull requests though, it seems like there is a possible plan to remove it altogether.

-  pylint replaced html_reporter with json_reporter because it's "prettier" 

- set _display to None

issue_title: %%bq query table view is broken
issue_body:
For example, run:

%bq query
SELECT * from `MyTable`

The first page is fine, but if you click "next page" button, it fails with "No Data" error message.

issue_title: Unhandled KeyError in ethpm.package

* Python: 3.6.5 **(Not in template)**
* OS: linux


### What was wrong?
Unhandled `KeyError`:

```python
w3 = Web3(Web3.IPCProvider("/home/bryant/.ethereum/testnet/geth.ipc"))
pm = PM(w3)  # `PM.attach(w3, 'pmp')` didn't seem to do anything

    ...:     "license": "MIT",                                                                                                           
    ...:     "authors": [                                                                                                                
    ...:       "Piper Merriam <pipermerriam@gmail.com>"                                                                                  
    ...:     ],                                                                                                                          
    ...:     "description": "Reusable contracts which implement a privileged 'owner' model for authorization.",                          
    ...:     "keywords": [                                                                                                               
    ...:       "authorization"
```

issue_title: Use new obfuscation mode to improve performance
issue_body:
* Encrypt
  - Compile source to code objects, or load .pyc as code objects
  - Iterate code object, co_code of each code object will be wrapped by an armor as the following format
`
    0 JUMP_ABSOLUTE            n = 3 + len(bytecode)

         ...                                   here it's obfuscation bytecode by des or some other algorithms

n + 3:
    0 LOAD_GLOBAL              ? (armor)
    3 CALL_FUNCTION            0
    6 POP_TOP
    7 JUMP_ABSOLUTE            0
`
* Decrypt
  - For each encrypted code object, first op is jump to n + 3
  - At n + 3, it will call a PyCFunction to decrypt those obfuscation bytecode
  - When it returns, jump to first op of really bytecode 

By this way, no import hooker, no setprofile/settrace required.  The performance will be improved remarkably.

issue_title: Make from_host synchronization part of task specification.
issue_body:
One of the problems with our `exit-and-resume` approach is that it is unclear who should synchronize results back from the server. 

More specifically, currently a task is prepared in a server-neutral manner. That is to say, when a task is translated, it can be either executed as

```
sos execute task_id -q host
```

or

```
sos execute task_id
```
on the host itself.

When the task is submitted and the main sos process quits, the task is still executed on the remote server. When SoS resumes and detects that the task is completed, it will sync the results back and continue. That is to say, **sos has to be re-run with the same `-q` option to complete the tasks**.

I am therefore wondering if we can make the retrieval of results easier so that the results would be available locally when the remote tasks are completed. This does not sound feasible because even if we code the `from_host` information in the job file, the remote host might not be able to copy the file to desktop without IP address and public key authentication.

issue_title: Coverage report with multiprocessing
issue_body:
We use a lot of multiprocessing stuff and it appears that lots of the code is not reported to be coverage. We will have to investigate how to improve coverage report. [A related ticket](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-cov/issues/139) might give us some hint.

Note that we might also have to adopt a `src` structure to make `pytest` happy for our tests.

issue_title: Auto-rerun and force signature
issue_body: Currently we re-run a cell after all its tasks are completed, this would allow a cell to continue execution. However, if `-s force`, re-run the cell will trigger re-run of the tasks, which is not what we want. Ignoring `-s force` during re-run is also problematic because users cannot re-run the cell manually.

issue_title: Specify writable non-project directories
issue_body:
It can be useful to require users to specify directories that a workflow can write to (other than the current project directory). For example, if a user would like to download some resource that could be shared by multiple projects, 

```
[10]
download(url, dest='/resource/hg19')
```

he would have to do

```
sos run script workflow -w /resource/hg19
```

where `-w` means `writable`.

It is hard to implement but in this way SoS can try to make sure no file would be changed or written outside of the project directory.

Note that it is easy to judge if files in `${intput}` and `${output}` would fail out of the current project directory, but it would be really hard to catch side effects of user scripts and programs. In addition, writing to `${temp}` is a common behavior of many applications and `${temp}` does not have to be system temporary directory.

issue_title: Ctrl-Shift-O for markdown cell
issue_body:
Current `Ctrl-Shift-O` only works for code cells and toggle a `report_output` tag. Because there can also be a need to hide markdown cells, I think we should let it toggle between `report_output` and `hide_output`, with default value for `markdown` being `report_output` (and switch to `hide_output`) and for `codecell` being`hide_output` (and switch to `report_output`).

Even further adjustment could be requested (e.g. `show_code`) but this change makes use of existing shortcut and can be easy to understood.

issue_title: Improve search function of sos website.
issue_body: The search function (at the top right corner of [SoS homepage](https://vatlab.github.io/SoS/)) is currently implemented by [tipue](http://www.tipue.com/search/) in static mode. More specifically, 1. Script [index_site.py](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/js/index_site.py) is run manually (with release) to index headers of all pages to create [tipuesearch_content.js](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/tipuesearch/tipuesearch_content.js). This process used [BeautifulSoup](https://www.crummy.com/software/BeautifulSoup/) to parse our pages. We grab only headers because we do not want to create huge `tipuesearch_content.js` that takes a lot of time and ram to load, and because it is better to target the most important items (headers) of the website. 2. When the search function is called, it opens [Search.html](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/search.html) and calls [tipuesearch](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/tipuesearch/tipuesearch.js#L12) to generate result page. Needed improvement: Because our headers (e.g. [this one](https://vatlab.github.io/SoS/doc/documentation/SoS_Step.html#Option-filetype-122) has anchors, we would like to display the results better in the search page and be able to jump directly to headers from the search page. Steps: 1. Improve [index_site.py](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/js/index_site.py) is run manually (with release) to generate better "text" to store the anchors. For example, instead of saving `Option filetype` for the aforementioned URL, we save `Option filetype #Option-filetype-122`. This can be achieved by better handling in the BeautifulSoup parsing. 2. Improve [tipuesearch](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/docs/tipuesearch/tipuesearch.js#L12) to better display the output. Basically, this function will find the item and display its context. We will need to change that part to display URLs from the anchors. That is to say, for matched text `filetype` with line `Option filetype #Option-filetype-122`, we display `Option filetype`.

issue_title: Task specific signature mode.
issue_body:
Right now, the tasks inherit signature mode from the sos instance. Even for PBS task engine, the signature mode is passed to the template and to the `sos execute` command.

I am wondering if there are cases that we want to keep signatures for main sos workflow, but use different signature mode for the tasks (something like `task: sig_mode='ignore'`. For example, the cluster might not like the multiple small files created by signatures (#991) and there is no need for signatures for large number of small tasks, or for production runs.

issue_title: Add support for Quiz Submissions API
Clean JSON responses at the requester level
issue_body:
Previously, we noticed `while(1);` prepended to a response during the file upload process. This breaks requests `Response.to_json()` method because technically an object of the form `while(1);{"key": "value"}` isn't valid JSON.

We resolved the issue by stripping `while(1);` out of the response at that stage in the uploader, but we left it there. I noticed that `while(1);` was prepended to a 401 response I got early. Rather than patching it in specific locations, it's probably a better idea to sanitize the response body directly before it leaves `Requester.request`.

issue_title: Add "troubleshooting" section for using Ray with TensorFlow documentation.
issue_body:
A common mistake when using `ray.experimental.TensorFlowVariables` to extract weights from `net1` and assign them to `net2` is that the variable names in `net1` and `net2` are different.

Consider the following setup.

```python
model1 = Model()
variables1 = ray.experimental.TensorFlowVariables(model1.loss, model1.sess)

model2 = Model()
variables2 = ray.experimental.TensorFlowVariables(model2.loss, model2.sess)
```

Then doing something like the following can fail.

```python
weights1 = variables1.get_weights()

variables2.set_weights(weights1)
```

If this happens, the first thing to check is that the variable names match. That is, we should have

```python
sorted(list(weights1.keys())) == sorted(list(weights2.keys()))
```

If that's not the case, then the problem may be that `Model1` and `Model2` are created in the same graph. It should be solvable by doing the following instead.

```python
with tf.Graph().as_default():
  model1 = Model()
  variables1 = ray.experimental.TensorFlowVariables(model1.loss, model1.sess)

with tf.Graph().as_default():
  model2 = Model()
  variables2 = ray.experimental.TensorFlowVariables(model2.loss, model2.sess)
```

issue_title: Remove all required tracers from GFS
issue_body: The Fortran code for GFS appears to never actually use any of the tracers (including specific humidity), other than to assign them into its tracer arrays and advect those arrays. We should refactor the code so that all tracer array preparation occurs in Python, and the Fortran code can accept zero-sized tracer arrays. Then GFS will use only the tracers registered with Sympl. CliMT could choose to register certain tracers with Sympl.

issue_title: Unintended object mutation
issue_body:
in caldav/davclient.py:
DAVClient.request lines 221-222:
        combined_headers = self.headers
        combined_headers.update(headers)
First line makes combined_headers to be a *reference* to self.headers, and therefore second line, in fact, updates self.headers with values from headers, affecting all subsequent requests.
The combined_headers should be a copy instead:
         combined_headers = dict(self.headers)
         combined_headers.update(headers)

issue_title: What should our generic low-level hostname resolution interface look like?
issue_body:
There are really two questions here:

* What should the API that users call for hostname resolution look like?

* If someone wants to register a custom resolver implementation, then should that be possible and how should it work?

Currently we just expose the standard (RFC-defined!) `getaddrinfo`/`getnameinfo` API, and we allow custom resolvers as long as they implement that interface.

Twisted makes some slightly different choices. Their `IHostnameResolver` interface:

* streams back responses incrementally rather than all at once (in trio terms: it returns an async iterable instead of an iterable),

* doesn't support named service lookup (like `getaddrinfo("127.0.0.1", "http")`, which looks up `http` in `/etc/services` to figure out that this means port 80 – they just say you have to always pass a port number)

* doesn't offer an `AI_CANONNAME` flag, or any of the other `AI_*` flags

* doesn't provide any API for reverse resolution, AFAICT (i.e. it's only `getaddrinfo`, not `getnameinfo`)

Rationale for the first point is [here](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/4362#comment:24), and for some others is [here](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/4362#comment:27).

I think the big question here is whether we want to deviate from `getaddrinfo` by streaming responses back. The problem with this is that one of the things `getaddrinfo` does is sort the responses, so a full proper standards-compliant version can't possibly stream responses. Of course, maybe you have a specific use case where you don't care about this, and you want to use your more efficient lookup code that streams out responses as they come in.

But ... even this doesn't really work, because it turns out that the main consumer of these things -- happy eyeballs -- is supposed to then sort the response *again* (see [RFC 6555 section 5.4](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6555#section-5.4)), so it needs all the DNS responses before it starts. Or technically I guess it only needs at least 1 IPv4 and 1 IPv6 response, and it doesn't need them until after the first connection fails/times-out, but this doesn't make much difference given that the way DNS works, you get all your IPv4 and IPv6 responses in 1 packet apiece. Basically, if you know that your local policy is (say) prefer-IPv6, and the IPv6 DNS response arrives before the IPv4 DNS response, then that does let you start the first connection attempt a few milliseconds earlier, or would if anyone implemented this... meh. I don't think this is very compelling.

And the other changes are basically just minor simplifications for simplification's sake. I don't think they're useful enough on their own to deviate from the `getaddrinfo` spec.

So basically, I think we should provide a `getaddrinfo` implementation, because it's well understood and works and in most cases it actually does exactly what you want. And then if you want different semantics, well... you can of course define your own abstraction, that switches between calling `trio.socket.getaddrinfo` or something else. But I don't think the reasons above are compelling enough for trio to provide that abstraction; we simply have no use for it, so we'd probably get it wrong. And you really need a well-defined set of semantics for everyone to write code against; it's fine if someone wants to use c-ares to make up some sort of sorta-kinda-not-really getaddrinfo semantics that's good enough for their use case, but that should be restricted to their code then, not be something they inject globally into all the other code in the process.

I guess in twisted the main advantage of having `IHostnameResolver` is that it lets you use resolvers like c-ares or `twisted.names` along with tricky code like their happy eyeballs implementation. (Which ignores section 5.4.) But in trio even reimplementing happy eyeballs isn't that hard...

issue_title: [discussion] What to do about cancel scopes / nurseries inside generators?
issue_body:
So here's a surprising thing that can happen in trio right now: if you have a generator (or async generator, doesn't matter) that yields inside a cancel scope, then the cancel scope remains in effect until you resume the generator and exit it.

For context managers, this is actually what you want, and we make use of this in lots of places, e.g.:
```python
@contextmanager
def fail_at(deadline):
    with move_on_at(deadline) as scope:
        yield scope
    ...

with fail_at(deadline):
    XX
```
So here we have: first `fail_at` runs for a bit, and it starts a cancel scope, and then it yields. And then the XX code runs *with the cancel scope in effect, even though `failt_at` is not on the stack*. And then `fail_at` is resumed, and it exits the cancel scope. A little confusing when you think about the details, but basically this is safe and useful and ultimately does what you'd expect.

However, in general this can cause strange behavior, and also crashes:

```python
import trio

def weird_generator():
    with trio.open_cancel_scope() as cs:
        yield cs

async def main():
    # hold onto a reference to prevent the gc from making this even more confusing:
    gen = weird_generator()
    for cs in gen:
        break
    cs.cancel()
    await trio.sleep(1)

trio.run(main)
```

Notice that even after we leave the loop and abandon the generator, the cancel scope is still in effect... and then the program crashes because trio detects that the cancel scope stack has become inconsistent (it tries to pop a scope that isn't at the top of the stack).

This all also applies to using nurseries inside async generators, because nurseries have an implicit cancel scope.

There are also some more generic issues with cleaning up (async) generators that call trio APIs after yielding, but that's another issue. The cancel scope leakage causes problems even while the generator is alive, and even if we had [PEP 533](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0533/) then this would still be an issue.

Really the ideal solution here would be to error out if someone tries to `yield` through a cancel scope, IFF they're not implementing a context manager.

I don't think there's any way to do this though without [PEP 521](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0521/), and even then we'd have to figure out somehow whether this particular generator is implementing a context manager. (I guess some annoying thread-local thing could work -- e.g. make `with trio.this_is_a_context_manager: ...` which sets some internal flag that disables the PEP 521-based checks.)

I don't currently have any other ideas how to improve this, except to put some prominent warning in the documentation.

issue_title: Usecase for nested run (and blocking outside runer)
issue_body:
Hi There, 

I'm working on adding some [Async support to IPython](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/11265), which basically convert the core to most of it being coroutine. I'm ending up with a use case where I'd actually like to call `trio.run()` from within `trio.run()` which you can't because of : 
```
if hasattr(GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT, "runner"):
        raise RuntimeError("Attempted to call run() from inside a run()")
```

Yes, I know, run inside run is a footgun, but in my case it's to call nested `IPython.embed()`, so you literally want to halt the outer embed, and start a new loop; 
I'm using the following that seem to work:

```python
from contextlib import contextmanager
 _sentinel = object()
@contextmanager
def new_context():
    import trio._core._run as tcr
    old_runner = getattr(tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT, 'runner', _sentinel)
    old_task = getattr(tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT, 'task', None)
    if old_runner is not _sentinel:
        del tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT.runner
    tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT.task = None
    yield
    if old_runner is not _sentinel:
        tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT.runner = old_runner
    tcr.GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT.task = old_task
```
Is there a better way ? 
If not, does that seem "Ok-ish", or do you see any other things I need to watch out for ? 

I know this is internals API, and I'm not requesting it to be stable, if they change, well so be it, and I'll mark the nested-embed in IPython as "Brittle and depends on some non-vetted code", but still really like to keep the functionality for some users.

issue_title: Add OS to usage stats collection
Default kwargs in bash apps are lost at command string format time
issue_body:
We made some changes in https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/commit/065f5d393b370f1d17d336039c3410467600ddd9 in order to avoid explicit checking and copying of default values to app functions at definition. This was made to support complex objects (eg from pandas) that do not support comparison to `Parameter.empty`.

In bash applications, we have two stages in which we compose the command string.
1. Execute the function with *args and **kwargs from call time
2. Auto format the return string with *args and **kwargs as a shorthand.

In the first phase, defaults from definition time are available, however in the second phase, we rely on formatting the string with just *args, and **kwargs missing the default values attached to the `func` object.

```python
@App('bash')
def foo(a=1, b=2)
    return "echo "a={a} b={b}"

foo(a=0, b=1).result() # This works
foo(b=1).result() # This will result in a KeyError as `a` is missing
```

issue_title: Schema validation error gives "Response object is too long."
issue_body:
Thanks for this library! We're using it quite heavily for our DevOps solution, and it has been working really well.

I just ran into a case where a resource failed the schema validation, but the CloudFormation console simply shows "Response object is too long." as the error message.

I think sending the entire schema where the validation failed might be too much. If we just cut off the  error message a little earlier, that would be great.

I just looked at where that message is coming from, and it appears to be a simple `str(e)` on the validation error, which resolves to [this big message](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/master/jsonschema/exceptions.py#L59). My suggested fix would be to catch the [ValidationError](https://python-jsonschema.readthedocs.io/en/latest/errors/#jsonschema.exceptions.ValidationError) and only pass along the `message` attribute.

If that sounds good, I would be happy to whip up a PR. Thanks!

issue_title: Evaluate Yamale as convenient or not for the long term
issue_body:
Right now we have 2 problems with Yamale:

1.  Doesn't produce the output we desire for validation errors.
2.  It doesn't print out lines/columns with the problem

For 1) we need to check how far we can customise the output errors by overriding the Validators.

For 2) we need to check:
a. whether Yamale supports line
b. if it doesn't support lines, whether we can fork and add support with a yaml parser including lines, like [ruamel](http://yaml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

issue_title: Application not cleared with m.solve(remote=True)
issue_body:
GEKKO needs to clear the application on the first cycle when remote=True else certain files become very large such as overrides.dbs and 127.0.0.1_gk_model0.info. Options also persist from prior applications. I tested this with m.server='http://127.0.0.1' and repeatedly solved a problem such as would happen with mhe or mpc. The INFO file has 3354 lines (all from 1 MV and 1 CV declared) and the overrides.dbs file is over 600 KB. Adding to the file each solve will make it large and will slow down the execution as all the lines are parsed.

I recommend two possible things:
1. Clear the application before uploading files. See line 313 of https://github.com/APMonitor/apm_python/blob/master/apm.py - the files are cleared by sending the command 'clear all'
2. Either replace the files each cycle or else only add things that change.

I'd like to run GEKKO for a couple weeks, months, or years with scheduled cycles and want to make sure that the application doesn't slow down when running either on a remote server or else on a localhost server.
